topics.title: Dissect and familiarize with an Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) to assess all critical functions, and generate a standard (IEC/ANSI/AAMI, etc.) safety, quality, and performance test report
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML607 - PCBML607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Dissect and familiarize with an Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) to assess all critical functions, and generate a standard (IEC/ANSI/AAMI, etc.) safety, quality, and performance test report

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Electrosurgical Unit (ESU)**, also termed a **High-Frequency (HF) Surgical Generator**, is a medical electronic device that uses high-frequency alternating current (typically **300 kHz to 5 MHz**) passed through biological tissue to achieve surgical effects such as **cutting (electrotomy)**, **coagulation (fulguration/desiccation)**, or a blended combination thereof. The principle relies on the localized concentration of electrical current producing controlled thermal destruction of tissue without causing neuromuscular stimulation, which occurs at lower frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (IEC 60601-2-2):** An Electrosurgical Unit is an *active surgical instrument* in which a high-frequency electrical current is used to cut and/or coagulate biological tissue. It is classified as a **Class IIb active medical device** under EU MDR 2017/745, requiring the highest level of safety verification.

> [!NOTE]
> The ESU operates on the principle of **electromagnetic energy → thermal energy** conversion at the active electrode tip. Current density (A/m²) is the key physical quantity that determines whether the effect is cutting or coagulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **magnifying glass focusing sunlight onto a single point of paper** — the energy is spread over a large area normally, but when focused, it becomes intense enough to burn. An ESU works on a similar principle, but instead of light, it focuses **electrical current**:

- **Active Electrode (Pencil/Forceps):** Acts like the focused lens — a tiny contact area with very **high current density** → extreme heat → tissue vaporization (CUTTING).
- **Return/Dispersive Electrode (Patient Plate):** Acts like the wide, unfocused area — a large surface area with **low current density** → no heating → safe return path.
- **Tissue Resistance (Impedance):** The biological resistance that converts current into heat: **P = I²R** (Joule Heating).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of an ESU as a *precision electrical scalpel*. While a traditional scalpel mechanically separates tissue, the ESU uses electricity to simultaneously cut and seal blood vessels — like a heated knife that cauterizes as it cuts.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Operating Frequency | **300 kHz – 5 MHz** (typical: 500 kHz – 2 MHz) | Avoids neuromuscular stimulation (50/60 Hz mains) |
| Power Output (Cut) | **50–400 W** into 500 Ω load | Adjustable, monitored |
| Power Output (Coag) | **30–150 W** into 500 Ω load | Lower than cut mode |
| Crest Factor (Cut) | **1.4 – 2.0** (quasi-sinusoidal) | Determines cutting purity |
| Crest Factor (Coag) | **5.0 – 8.0** (pulsed/burst) | Determines coag quality |
| Load Resistance | **50–2000 Ω** (test standard: 500 Ω) | IEC 60601-2-2 reference |
| Leakage Current Limit | **Patient Aux Current: ≤ 0.01 mA** (normal), **0.05 mA** (fault) | IEC 60601-1 |
| Earth Leakage | **≤ 0.5 mA** (normal), **≤ 1 mA** (single fault) | Class I devices |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Architecture of a Modern ESU

A modern solid-state ESU consists of the following functional subsystems:

1. **Mains Input & EMI Filter** — Line filter, surge protection, isolation transformer primary.
2. **HV DC Power Supply** — Rectified + filtered DC bus (typically **+200 V to +500 V DC**) feeding the HF oscillator.
3. **HF Oscillator/Generator Module** — Power MOSFET/IGBT-based resonant or class-D amplifier at **f = 300 kHz – 5 MHz**.
4. **Output Power Stage & Matching Network** — Step-up transformer + LC tank for impedance matching to tissue load.
5. **Output Power Sensing Circuit** — RMS current and voltage transducers (CTs and VTs) feeding the **microcontroller/DSP** for closed-loop power regulation.
6. **Mode Selection & Control Logic** — Front-panel rotary switch, footswitch input, monopolar/bipolar selector.
7. **Patient Return Electrode Monitoring (REM/ARM/CQM)** — Compares split-plate return electrode impedance; cuts power if **> 75 Ω imbalance** or if plate is missing.
8. **Microcontroller + Display Module** — LCD/LED, audio tones, error/fault indicators.
9. **Safety Interlocks & Alarms** — Watchdog timer, thermal shutdown, over-current protection, fault relays.
10. **Output Relays & High-Voltage Relays** — Mechanical or solid-state relays isolating the patient when in standby.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Patient Return Electrode Monitoring (REM)** circuit — branded as ARM (Aspen Labs), CQM (Conmed), or NESSY (Erbe) — is the single most critical safety feature. It continuously monitors the impedance of a **split (dual) return pad** and disables HF output if the contact quality degrades (e.g., pad detaches during surgery).

## 2.2 Operating Modes of an ESU

| Mode | Waveform | Crest Factor (CF) | Tissue Effect | Typical Use |
|------|----------|-------------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Pure Cut (CUT)** | Continuous 500 kHz sinusoid | **1.4 – 2.0** | Vaporization/electrotomy | Clean incisions |
| **Blend 1 (CUT + 20% coag duty)** | Modulated 80% duty | 2.0 – 2.5 | Cut with light hemostasis | General surgery |
| **Blend 2** | Modulated 60% duty | 2.5 – 3.0 | Cut with moderate hemostasis | Vascular tissue |
| **Blend 3** | Modulated 40% duty | 3.0 – 4.0 | Slow cut, strong hemostasis | Liver, dense tissue |
| **Desiccation (Coag – Contact)** | Continuous low-voltage | 1.5 – 2.0 | Slow dehydration/coag | Bipolar forceps, vessel sealing |
| **Fulguration (Spray Coag)** | Pulsed high-voltage bursts | **5.0 – 8.0** | Superficial coagulation | Surface bleeders |
| **Bipolar** | Lower power (≤ 50 W) | ~1.5 | Precise, low-thermal spread | Neurosurgery, microsurgery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crest Factor (CF)** is defined as the ratio of peak voltage to RMS voltage: **CF = V_peak / V_rms**. A high CF waveform (coagulation) produces high peak voltages that arc through tissue, while a low CF waveform (cut) produces a smooth sine wave that vaporizes cells cleanly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mathematically essential** for the KTU ESE, lab viva, and Module 2 practical examination. Memorize the table — it is high-yield.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $$P = I^2 R$$ | Joule heating power dissipated in tissue | $P$ in W, $I$ in A, $R$ in Ω |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Power in terms of load voltage | $V$ in V (RMS) |
| 3 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Apparent power delivered to load | Both RMS quantities |
| 4 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | **Current density** at electrode | $J$ in A/m², $A$ in m² |
| 5 | $\text{CF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{rms}}}$ | **Crest factor** of HF waveform | Dimensionless; CUT ≈ 1.4–2, COAG ≈ 5–8 |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{cut}} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{CF}}$ | Cutting efficiency is inversely related to CF | Empirical, dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant tank frequency (HF generator) | $L$ in H, $C$ in F |
| 8 | $Z_{\text{load}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{rms}}}{I_{\text{rms}}}$ | Tissue impedance (50–2000 Ω typical) | Ω; reference 500 Ω for testing |
| 9 | $E_{\text{leakage}} \leq 0.5 \text{ mA}$ | Earth leakage current limit (normal condition) | Class I, IEC 60601-1 |
| 10 | $I_{\text{patient aux}} \leq 0.01 \text{ mA}$ | Patient auxiliary current (normal, Type CF) | IEC 60601-1, Type CF |
| 11 | $I_{\text{patient aux}} \leq 0.05 \text{ mA}$ | Patient auxiliary current (single-fault, Type CF) | IEC 60601-1 |
| 12 | $P_{\text{disp pad}} = \dfrac{I^2 R_{\text{body}}}{A_{\text{pad}}}$ | Power density under dispersive electrode | W/m²; < 1.5 W/cm² safe limit |
| 13 | $\tau_{\text{therm}} = R_{\text{th}} C_{\text{th}}$ | Thermal time constant of electrode-tissue interface | s; ~0.5–5 s typical |
| 14 | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat energy to raise tissue temperature | $Q$ in J, $c = 4180$ J/kg·K for water |
| 15 | $f_{\text{REM alarm}} \rightarrow \text{SHUTDOWN}$ | Return electrode monitoring threshold | Activate at $R_{\text{split imbalance}} > 75\ \Omega$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ESU design principles extend into:
- **Industrial RF plasma cutting/welding** — same HF principle for non-medical cutting.
- **RFID ablation and tumor ablation** — RF ablation catheters in interventional oncology.
- **Veterinary surgery** — identical generator architecture.
- **Aerospace welding** — high-frequency plasma welding of titanium alloys uses the same impedance-matching concepts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Density at the Dispersive Electrode (Patient Return Plate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **single most asked conceptual question** in KTU viva and lab reports. You will be tested on it.

**Problem Statement:** A monopolar ESU delivers **P_total = 200 W** of HF power to a 500 Ω tissue load. The current returns through a dispersive (return) pad of effective area **A_pad = 100 cm² = 0.01 m²**. Compute the power density at the return electrode and verify it is within the safety limit of **1.5 W/cm²**.

**Step 1 — Compute load current using Ohm's law:**

$$P = \frac{V^2}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{P \cdot R}$$

$$V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{200 \times 500} = \sqrt{100{,}000} = 316.23\ \text{V}_{\text{rms}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the load current:**

$$I_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}}}{R} = \frac{316.23}{500} = 0.6325\ \text{A}_{\text{rms}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute current density at the return electrode:**

$$J = \frac{I}{A_{\text{pad}}} = \frac{0.6325\ \text{A}}{100\ \text{cm}^2} = 6.325\ \text{mA/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the power density at the return electrode:**

$$\sigma_{\text{pad}} = J^2 \cdot \rho_{\text{tissue}}$$

But for direct calculation, we use the equivalent formula:

$$\sigma_{\text{pad}} = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{pad}}} = \frac{200\ \text{W}}{100\ \text{cm}^2} = 2.0\ \text{W/cm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the safety limit:**

$$\sigma_{\text{pad}} = 2.0\ \text{W/cm}^2 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sigma_{\text{limit}} = 1.5\ \text{W/cm}^2$$

**Result:** $2.0 > 1.5$, therefore the configuration **EXCEEDS** the safe power density. The clinical fix is to either (a) use a larger pad (≥ 135 cm²) or (b) reduce power to **P < 150 W**.

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{pad, required}} \leq 1.5\ \text{W/cm}^2 \Rightarrow A_{\text{pad, min}} = \frac{P}{1.5} = \frac{200}{1.5} = 133.3\ \text{cm}^2}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Crest Factor and Mode Identification

**Problem:** An ESU outputs a waveform with **V_peak = 800 V** and **V_rms = 200 V**. Identify the operating mode and justify.

**Step 1 — Apply the crest factor formula:**

$$\text{CF} = \frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{rms}}} = \frac{800}{200} = 4.0$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the reference ranges:**

| Range | Mode |
|-------|------|
| 1.4 – 2.0 | Pure CUT |
| 2.0 – 3.0 | Blend 1 / Blend 2 |
| 3.0 – 4.0 | Blend 3 / Coag (transition) |
| 5.0 – 8.0 | Spray Coag / Fulguration |

**Result:** CF = 4.0 corresponds to **Blend 3 (heavy cut with strong hemostasis)**, or transition coagulation. A technician would map this on a digital oscilloscope trace to verify the manufacturer spec.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Leakage Current Calculation Under Single Fault

**Problem:** During a hipot test at **1500 V AC** for 1 second on the patient circuit, the measured leakage is **I_meas = 0.04 mA**. Verify Type CF compliance per IEC 60601-1.

**Step 1 — Recall the standard limit:**

$$I_{\text{patient, single fault, Type CF}} \leq 0.05\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compare:**

$$I_{\text{meas}} = 0.04\ \text{mA} < 0.05\ \text{mA}\ \text{limit} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PASS}$$

**Step 3 — Earth leakage check (Class I, normal condition):**

$$I_{\text{earth, NC}} \leq 0.5\ \text{mA}$$

If the measured value is **I_earth = 0.3 mA** → **PASS**.

If, however, the **PE (protective earth) wire is opened (single fault condition):**

$$I_{\text{earth, SFC}} \leq 1.0\ \text{mA}$$

---

| 4 | Earth leakage (SFC) | IEC 60601-1 | Earth open | $\leq 1.0$ mA | — | ☐ |

| 15 | Alarm/indicator function | IEC 60601-1 | Force faults sequentially | All alarms triggered | — | ☐ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Power Output Verification

**Question (KTU ESE Pattern):** An ESU is set to deliver **200 W in CUT mode** into a **500 Ω** non-inductive resistive test load. The oscilloscope shows $V_{\text{peak}} = 632\ \text{V}$ and $V_{\text{rms}} = 447\ \text{V}$. Verify compliance and compute the crest factor.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the actual delivered power:**

$$P_{\text{meas}} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}}^2}{R} = \frac{(447)^2}{500} = \frac{199{,}809}{500} = 399.6\ \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with setpoint:**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{|P_{\text{meas}} - P_{\text{set}}|}{P_{\text{set}}} \times 100\% = \frac{|399.6 - 200|}{200} \times 100\% = 99.8\%$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{This FAILS the } \pm 20\% \text{ tolerance.}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the crest factor:**

$$\text{CF} = \frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{rms}}} = \frac{632}{447} = 1.414$$

**Interpretation:** CF = 1.414 corresponds to a **pure sine wave (CF_ideal = √2 = 1.414)**, confirming the device is in CUT mode. The power is overshooting — likely a calibration drift in the power feedback loop.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define an Electrosurgical Unit. State any four clinical applications of an ESU. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

An **Electrosurgical Unit (ESU)** is a high-frequency (300 kHz – 5 MHz) electrosurgical generator that uses alternating current passed through biological tissue to produce controlled thermal effects — cutting, coagulation, or a blend thereof — for surgical use. The high frequency is chosen specifically to avoid neuromuscular stimulation (which occurs at 50/60 Hz).

**Four Clinical Applications:**
1. **General Surgery** — incision, hemostasis, and dissection of soft tissues.
2. **Gynecology** — laparoscopic tubal ligation, hysteroscopic procedures.
3. **ENT (Ear, Nose, Throat)** — tonsillectomy, septoplasty, turbinate reduction.
4. **Dermatology & Plastic Surgery** — mole excision, scar revision, skin graft harvesting.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Orthopedics — cutting soft tissue around joints, arthroscopy.

---

### **Q2.** What is **Patient Return Electrode Monitoring (REM/CQM/ARM)**? Why is it critical for monopolar ESU safety? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Patient Return Electrode Monitoring (REM)** — also called **ARM** (Aspen Return Monitor), **CQM** (Conmed Quality Monitor), or **NESSY** (Neutral Electrode Safety System) — is a continuous safety circuit in monopolar ESUs that monitors the **impedance (or contact quality)** of the **split (dual-section) dispersive return pad** placed on the patient's body.

The pad is divided into two electrically isolated halves. The ESU measures the impedance of each half. If the two impedances differ by more than **~75 Ω** (or if total contact impedance exceeds a threshold), it indicates the pad has lifted, dried out, or lost contact. The REM circuit then **disables the HF output within 5 seconds** and triggers an audible/visual alarm.

**Why Critical:** Without REM, a partially detached return pad would concentrate current over a smaller area, dramatically increasing current density and causing **HF burns at the return site** — a known cause of preventable surgical injury and a recurring medico-legal issue in electrosurgery.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**[A] (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a modern solid-state Electrosurgical Unit. Identify and describe the function of **at least six major sub-modules**. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**[A] (b)** Describe the **dissection procedure** you would follow to open an ESU, document its internal architecture, and perform a visual and mechanical inspection per **IEC 60601-1**. List at least 8 specific inspection checkpoints that must be recorded. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

A modern solid-state ESU consists of the following functional sub-modules (see Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Mains Input & EMI Filter (230 V AC, 50 Hz):** Suppresses line-borne electromagnetic interference; includes surge suppression (MOV) and a line filter to keep HF noise from leaking back into the mains.
2. **HV DC Power Supply:** Bridge rectifier + capacitor filter + linear/switch-mode regulator producing a stable HV DC bus (typically +400 V) for the HF oscillator.
3. **HF Oscillator & Power Amplifier:** A class-D or push-pull MOSFET/IGBT resonant inverter generating **f = 300 kHz to 2 MHz** sinusoidal/pulsed HF output.
4. **Output Matching Network & HF Transformer:** LC tank + step-up transformer matching the high-impedance tissue load to the low-impedance power transistors; also provides galvanic isolation (typically > 4 kV).
5. **Output Power Sensing:** Current transformers (CTs) and voltage dividers feed RMS values to a microcontroller/DSP for **closed-loop power regulation** (constant-power mode).
6. **Mode Selection & Footswitch Decoder:** Front-panel rotary switch + footswitch input allows selection of CUT/COAG/BLEND; footswitch enables output only when both pedals or active switches are engaged.
7. **Patient Return Electrode Monitor (REM):** Continuously monitors the dual-section return pad impedance; disables HF output if imbalance > 75 Ω.
8. **Microcontroller & Display:** LCD/LED display of set power, mode, error codes; audio tone for each mode.
9. **Safety Interlocks:** Watchdog timer, thermal shutdown, over-voltage protection, and isolation monitoring.

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Dissection Procedure:**

1. **Power down and unplug the ESU.** Allow 5 minutes for capacitor discharge. Wear an anti-static wrist strap.
2. **Photograph the exterior** from all four sides. Note make, model, serial number, manufacture date, and IEC classification marking (Class I, Type CF, IPX1, etc.).
3. **Remove top cover** using a Phillips/Pozidriv screwdriver. Document the screw type and torque.
4. **Photograph the internal layout** — top view showing all PCBs, transformers, and cable harnesses.
5. **Identify and label each major sub-assembly** — HV power supply, oscillator PCB, output stage, control PCB, display board, footswitch connector, REM module.
6. **Trace the mains path:** inlet → fuse → line filter → power switch → isolation transformer primary.
7. **Trace the HF output path:** oscillator → driver → power stage → HF transformer → output relay → patient connectors.
8. **Document connector types and pin assignments** between modules.

**8+ Visual & Mechanical Inspection Checkpoints (per IEC 60601-1 §6):**

| # | Checkpoint | Criteria |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Enclosure integrity | No cracks, dents, or burn marks |
| 2 | Labeling and markings | All labels legible, model/serial/rating intact |
| 3 | Mains plug & cord | No damage; insulation intact; correct plug type |
| 4 | Fuse ratings | Fuses match specification |
| 5 | PCB condition | No burnt components, no bulging capacitors, no corrosion |
| 6 | Solder joints | No cold joints, no solder bridges (visual with 10× loupe) |
| 7 | Wire dress | No pinched/strained wires, no bare conductors |
| 8 | Connectors and sockets | All connectors secure; no bent pins |
| 9 | Patient connectors | Active and return pad connectors intact and clean |
| 10 | Footswitch cable | No kinks or cuts; strain relief intact |
| 11 | Display and indicators | All segments working; backlight functional |
| 12 | Audible alarm | All alarm tones functional |

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)**

**[B] (a)** Define **crest factor**. Compute and interpret the crest factor for the following ESU measurements and identify the corresponding operating mode: **[7 Marks]**
- (i) V_peak = 350 V, V_rms = 250 V
- (ii) V_peak = 1200 V, V_rms = 200 V
- (iii) V_peak = 707 V, V_rms = 500 V

**[B] (b)** A service engineer is preparing a **standard safety, quality, and performance test report** for an ESU as part of a hospital's annual preventive maintenance program. Describe the **structure and contents** of such a report, citing the relevant **IEC and AAMI standards** for each section. Mention any **15 test items** in a tabular form. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Definition:** **Crest Factor (CF)** is the ratio of the peak voltage to the RMS voltage of an AC waveform:

$$\text{CF} = \frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{rms}}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** number that quantifies how "peaked" a waveform is. A pure sine wave has CF = √2 ≈ 1.414. ESU operating modes are classified by CF:

| Mode | CF Range |
|------|----------|
| Pure CUT | 1.4 – 2.0 |
| Blend 1/2/3 | 2.0 – 4.0 |
| Desiccation (Coag) | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Spray/Fulguration | 5.0 – 8.0 |

**Calculations:**

**(i)** $V_{\text{peak}} = 350\ \text{V},\ V_{\text{rms}} = 250\ \text{V}$:

$$\text{CF} = \frac{350}{250} = 1.40$$

→ **Pure CUT mode** (boundary case, very close to ideal sine wave).

**(ii)** $V_{\text{peak}} = 1200\ \text{V},\ V_{\text{rms}} = 200\ \text{V}$:

$$\text{CF} = \frac{1200}{200} = 6.0$$

→ **Spray Coag / Fulguration mode** (very high peak voltages; arc-based coagulation).

**(iii)** $V_{\text{peak}} = 707\ \text{V},\ V_{\text{rms}} = 500\ \text{V}$:

$$\text{CF} = \frac{707}{500} = 1.414$$

→ **Pure CUT mode** (theoretical ideal sine wave: CF = √2).

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Structure of a Standard ESU Test Report:**

A standard ESU safety, quality, and performance test report (compliant with **IEC 60601-1**, **IEC 60601-2-2**, and **AAMI HF18 / NS4**) must contain the following sections:

1. **Cover Page** — Hospital name, device make/model/serial, date of test, engineer name, signature.
2. **Device Identification** — Manufacturer, model, serial, asset number, location, manufacture date, last service date.
3. **Reference Standards** — IEC 60601-1, IEC 60601-2-2, AAMI HF18, manufacturer service manual.
4. **Visual & Mechanical Inspection Checklist** — Per IEC 60601-1 §6.
5. **Electrical Safety Tests** — Earth continuity, earth leakage (NC + SFC), enclosure leakage, patient leakage (Type CF), patient auxiliary current, dielectric strength.
6. **Performance Tests** — Output power accuracy (CUT and COAG at multiple settings), HF leakage, crest factor verification, waveform integrity.
7. **Safety System Verification** — REM/ARM function, footswitch activation delay, alarm/indicator function, mode switching.
8. **Test Instruments Used** — ESA620 / Rigel 288 / Fluke QA-ES II electrical safety analyzer, oscilloscope (≥ 100 MHz, 100× HF probe), HF dummy load (500 Ω non-inductive), digital wattmeter.
9. **Results Table** — Tabulated test results with pass/fail flags.
10. **Non-conformity & Recommendations** — Any deviation and corrective action.
11. **Calibration Certificates** — Of all test instruments used.
12. **Conclusion & Authorization** — Device "Fit for Use" / "Conditionally Fit" / "Unfit".
13. **Appendices** — Raw oscilloscope screenshots, instrument calibration certificates, equipment photographs.

**15 Test Items (Tabular Form):**

| # | Test | Standard | Limit / Criterion |
|---|------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Visual inspection | IEC 60601-1 §6 | All labels & enclosure intact |
| 2 | Mechanical inspection | IEC 60601-1 | No damaged controls/connectors |
| 3 | Earth continuity | IEC 60601-1 | $R \leq 0.2\ \Omega$ at 25 A |
| 4 | Earth leakage (NC) | IEC 60601-1 | $\leq 0.5$ mA |
| 5 | Earth leakage (SFC) | IEC 60601-1 | $\leq 1.0$ mA |
| 6 | Enclosure leakage (NC) | IEC 60601-1 | $\leq 0.1$ mA |
| 7 | Enclosure leakage (SFC) | IEC 60601-1 | $\leq 0.5$ mA |
| 8 | Patient leakage (NC, Type CF) | IEC 60601-1 | $\leq 0.01$ mA |
| 9 | Patient leakage (SFC, Type CF) | IEC 60601-1 | $\leq 0.05$ mA |
| 10 | Dielectric strength (hipot) | IEC 60601-1 | 1500 V AC, 1 min, no breakdown |
| 11 | Output power accuracy | IEC 60601-2-2 §6 | $\pm 20\%$ of setpoint at 500 Ω |
| 12 | HF leakage to patient | IEC 60601-2-2 §7 | $\leq 150$ mA RMS |
| 13 | Crest factor (CUT) | AAMI HF18 | $1.4 \leq \text{CF} \leq 2.0$ |
| 14 | Crest factor (COAG) | AAMI HF18 | $5.0 \leq \text{CF} \leq 8.0$ |
| 15 | REM function | IEC 60601-2-2 §6.8 | Alarm & shutdown < 5 s on pad lift |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Crest Factor" with "Power"** — they are independent quantities. CF is shape-based; power is magnitude-based.
> 2. **Always specify the load resistance** (typically 500 Ω) when stating output power — power into a different load is meaningless in ESU testing.
> 3. **REM/CQM/ARM is NOT the same as Earth monitoring** — students frequently conflate these two distinct safety circuits.
> 4. **Type CF vs Type B matters** — ESUs are typically Type CF (most stringent patient protection). Mixing up the leakage limits will cost you 3–4 marks easily.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify "Normal Condition" vs "Single Fault Condition"** in leakage tests is the single most common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Reporting the test in tabular form** is essential — prose-only answers lose organization marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 LAST-MINUTE REVISION (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

- ⚡ **ESU** = High-frequency (300 kHz – 5 MHz) electrosurgical generator for cutting & coagulation.
- ⚡ **Monopolar** uses an active tip + dispersive return pad (REM-monitored). **Bipolar** uses two forceps tips — no return pad.
- ⚡ **Crest Factor (CF) = V_peak / V_rms**: CUT ≈ 1.4–2.0, COAG (spray) ≈ 5–8.
- ⚡ **Power = I²R** (Joule heating); **Power density at pad = P / A_pad ≤ 1.5 W/cm²**.
- ⚡ **REM/ARM/CQM** monitors split return pad; alarm if imbalance > 75 Ω; shutdown < 5 s.
- ⚡ **Key Standards:** **IEC 60601-1** (general safety), **IEC 60601-2-2** (HF surgical equipment), **AAMI HF18** (US performance).
- ⚡ **Patient leakage limit (Type CF):** ≤ 0.01 mA (normal), ≤ 0.05 mA (single fault).
- ⚡ **Earth leakage limit (Class I):** ≤ 0.5 mA (NC), ≤ 1.0 mA (SFC).
- ⚡ **Test load:** **500 Ω non-inductive** is the standard reference load.
- ⚡ **Power accuracy:** ±20% of setpoint at 500 Ω.
- ⚡ **Dielectric (hipot) test:** 1500 V AC for 1 second — no breakdown.
- ⚡ **Footswitch activation delay** should be **< 50 ms**.
- ⚡ **Dissection procedure:** Photograph, document, identify modules, **do not power on with cover removed** (high-voltage hazard).
- ⚡ **Test report must include:** device ID, reference standards, test instrument calibration, pass/fail matrix, conclusion, signature.
- ⚡ **Active electrode** = small area, high current density → CUT. **Dispersive pad** = large area, low current density → SAFE return.
- ⚡ **Common ESU failures** (memorize for viva): REM alarm malfunction, footswitch stuck, power drift, broken patient cable, blown fuse, display failure, HV capacitor aging.
- ⚡ **Always discharge capacitors** before touching internal circuits — the HV DC bus can be **lethal even after power-off**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dissect and conduct testing on a Defibrillator using a Defibrillator Analyser to ensure it meets performance specifications, and produce a standard (IEC/ANSI/AAMI, etc.) safety, quality, and performance test report
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML607 - PCBML607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Dissect and conduct testing on a Defibrillator using a Defibrillator Analyser to ensure it meets performance specifications, and produce a standard (IEC/ANSI/AAMI, etc.) safety, quality, and performance test report

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Defibrillator** is a life-critical electromedical device (EMD) that delivers a controlled, high-energy, direct-current (DC) electrical shock to the myocardium through the chest wall or directly to the exposed heart, with the therapeutic objective of depolarizing a critical mass of myocardial cells and terminating life-threatening arrhythmias such as **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)** and **pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT)**. Modern defibrillators also offer **synchronized cardioversion** (for stable VT, atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, SVT) and **external pacing** functions.

A **Defibrillator Analyser** (also called a **Defibrillator Tester** or **External Pacer/Defib Analyzer**, e.g., Fluke Impulse 7000DP, Metron DA-2006, Netech Delta 1500) is a precision measurement instrument that emulates a patient load (typically selectable from **25 Ω, 50 Ω, 75 Ω, 100 Ω, 125 Ω, 150 Ω, 175 Ω, 200 Ω**) and quantitatively measures the defibrillator's output energy, voltage, current, charge time, discharge time, synchronization delay, and pulse waveform integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBML607 – Module 2):** This experiment validates that the defibrillator under test (DUT) complies with **IEC 60601-2-4:2010/AMD1:2018** (Particular requirements for the basic safety and essential performance of cardiac defibrillators), **IEC 60601-1:2005+AMD1:2012+AMD2:2020** (General requirements for basic safety and essential performance), **AAMI DF80:2015** (Defibrillator energy delivery), **ANSI/AAMI HE75:2009 (R2018)** (Human factors), and **IEC 62353:2014** (Medical electrical equipment – Recurrent test and test after repair of ME equipment).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a defibrillator as a **"reset button" for a chaotic heart**. When the heart muscle quivers chaotically (fibrillation), it cannot pump blood. The defibrillator acts like a large camera-flash capacitor — it **stores energy slowly from a battery**, then releases it all in a few **milliseconds** through paddle electrodes placed on the patient's chest. This massive electrical "shout" momentarily stops every heart cell simultaneously, after which the heart's natural pacemaker (SA node) takes over with a normal rhythm.

The **Defibrillator Analyser** is the **"phantom patient"** — a controlled, predictable load that stands in for a real human chest. Just like a multimeter checks whether a battery delivers its rated voltage, the analyzer checks whether the defibrillator really delivers its claimed **360 joules** in the right shape, at the right time, and within regulatory safety limits.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Standard adult energy dose (per AHA 2020 ACLS):** **200 J** (biphasic), escalating to **300 J → 360 J** if refractory.
- **Standard monophasic dose:** **360 J**.
- **Standard pediatric dose:** **2–4 J/kg**.
- **Transthoracic impedance range:** **25 Ω to 200 Ω** (adult mean ≈ **70–90 Ω**).
- **Charge time to 360 J (mains + battery):** **≤ 7 s**, with **≤ 15 s** from a depleted battery.
- **Synchronization delay:** **≤ 60 ms** from R-wave peak.
- **Maximum patient leakage current (BF/CF applied part, normal condition):** **0.5 mA** (CF – defibrillation-proof type CF).
- **Defibrillator energy accuracy tolerance:** **±15 %** or **±3 J**, whichever is greater (per AAMI DF80).

> [!NOTE]
> **CF-Type Applied Part** is mandatory for direct cardiac contact or defibrillation-proof use. CF stands for **Cardiac Floating** — the highest protection grade against leakage current.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Defibrillator

A typical defibrillator consists of six functional subsystems:

1. **High-Voltage Capacitor Bank:** Stores energy $E_C = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$. Typical values: **C = 32–200 µF**, charged to **≤ 2000–2000 V** for monophasic, **≤ 1700–2000 V** for biphasic.
2. **High-Voltage Charging Circuit:** A DC–DC converter (or step-up transformer + rectifier) drawing from the internal battery or mains.
3. **Discharge Switching Network (H-Bridge):** For monophasic: a single high-voltage switch (SCR/IGBT). For biphasic: a four-switch H-bridge that reverses polarity mid-pulse.
4. **Patient Isolation / Paddle Electrodes:** Either hand-held paddles, self-adhesive pads, or internal spoons/paddles.
5. **ECG Acquisition & Synchronization Module:** Detects R-waves for synchronized cardioversion.
6. **Microprocessor Controller & User Interface:** Sets energy, controls charge/discharge, displays waveforms, stores events.

### 2.2 Waveform Taxonomy

| Waveform | Description | Peak Current (50 Ω) | Energy Setting |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monophasic Damped Sine (MDS)** | Classic Lown waveform – RLC damped oscillation | ~30 A at 360 J | 200, 300, 360 J |
| **Monophasic Truncated Exponential (MTE)** | Capacitor discharge, electronically truncated | ~35 A at 360 J | 200, 300, 360 J |
| **Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE)** | Two phases, 2nd phase reversed polarity, exponential decay | ~15–20 A at 200 J | 120, 150, 200 J |
| **Rectilinear Biphasic (RLB)** | Constant-current first phase, truncated exponential second | ~12–16 A at 200 J | 120, 150, 200 J |
| **Pulsed Biphasic** | Biphasic with multiple chopped segments | Variable | Variable |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stored capacitor energy | $E_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J (joule) | Measured **before** discharge |
| 2 | Delivered energy (into load R) | $E_d = \int_{0}^{t_{end}} \dfrac{v(t)^2}{R}\, dt$ | J | True clinical measure |
| 3 | Discharged efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_d}{E_s} \times 100$ | % | Typically 70–95 % |
| 4 | Transthoracic impedance | $Z = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{I_{peak}}$ | Ω | Resistance at pulse onset |
| 5 | RC discharge time constant | $\tau = R C$ | s | Time to drop to 37 % |
| 6 | Charge time constant (Cap charger) | $t_{ch} \approx \dfrac{C V_{max}}{I_{ch}} \cdot k$ | s | $k$ = charger efficiency ≈ 1.2–1.5 |
| 7 | Synchronization delay | $\Delta t_{sync} = t_{R-peak} - t_{shock-onset}$ | ms | Must be ≤ 60 ms |
| 8 | Leakage current (BF applied part) | $I_{leak,NC}$ | mA | Limit ≤ 0.5 mA NC, ≤ 1 mA SFC |
| 9 | Patient aux current (CF) | $I_{aux}$ | mA | Limit ≤ 0.01 mA NC (10 µA) |
| 10 | Energy tolerance band | $\Delta E = \pm 15\% \text{ or } \pm 3\text{ J}$ | J | Per AAMI DF80 |
| 11 | Peak voltage limit (defib-proof) | $V_{peak} \leq 5\,\text{kV}$ into 100 Ω | V | Per IEC 60601-2-4 § 101 |
| 12 | Maximum pulse duration | $t_{pulse} \leq 60$ ms total | ms | Sum of both phases |
| 13 | First-phase tilt | $\text{Tilt}_1 = 1 - \dfrac{V_{2,end}}{V_{1,start}}$ | % | Biphasic: typically 60–70 % |
| 14 | Mean current over phase | $I_{mean} = \dfrac{1}{t_{phase}}\int v(t)/R\,dt$ | A | Used to compare waveforms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always replace the pipe symbol `|` in math expressions with `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table intact (e.g., write $\vert V \vert$ as $\lvert V \rvert$).

### 2.4 Operational Logic — Why and How

- **Why biphasic?** Biphasic waveforms are more effective at lower energies (~150 J) because the second reversed phase **removes residual charge** from the myocardium, lowering the defibrillation threshold and reducing myocardial injury.
- **How the analyzer works?** It uses a high-precision **non-inductive resistive load** (a bank of power resistors with low temperature coefficient, e.g., **wirewound or foil resistors** rated for kW peaks). A **high-voltage divider** scales the voltage to a digitizer, and the instrument numerically integrates $E_d = \int v(t)^2/R\, dt$ to display delivered energy in joules.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Compliance testing of defibrillators is mandatory under **Medical Device Rules 2017 of India (CDSCO)**, **EU MDR 2017/745**, **US FDA 21 CFR 870.5300**, and **ISO 13485** quality systems. Biomedical engineers in **hospital clinical engineering departments**, **third-party calibration labs (NABL accredited)**, and **medical device manufacturers** perform this test **annually**, after **every repair**, and after **firmware/hardware upgrades**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stored Energy vs. Delivered Energy

Let a capacitor $C$ be charged to voltage $V_s$. The stored electrostatic energy is:

$$
E_s = \frac{1}{2} C V_s^{2}
$$

When discharged through load $R$, the capacitor voltage decays exponentially:

$$
v(t) = V_s \, e^{-t/RC}
$$

The instantaneous current is $i(t) = v(t)/R = (V_s/R) \, e^{-t/RC}$. The instantaneous power delivered to $R$ is:

$$
p(t) = \frac{v(t)^2}{R} = \frac{V_s^{2}}{R} \, e^{-2t/RC}
$$

The **delivered energy** $E_d$ is the time-integral of $p(t)$ from $0$ to $\infty$ (practical $t_{end} \approx 5\tau$):

$$
E_d = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{V_s^{2}}{R} \, e^{-2t/RC} \, dt
$$

Let $u = 2t/RC$, so $du = (2/RC)\, dt$ → $dt = (RC/2)\, du$:

$$
E_d = \frac{V_s^{2}}{R} \cdot \frac{RC}{2} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u}\, du
$$

Since $\int_0^\infty e^{-u}\, du = 1$:

$$
E_d = \frac{V_s^{2} \, C}{2} = \frac{1}{2} C V_s^{2} = E_s
$$

> **Conclusion (ideal R discharge):** All stored energy is theoretically delivered to a resistive load. Real defibrillators have **internal losses** ($R_{int}$, switch on-resistance $R_{sw}$, inductance $L$ of leads), so $E_d < E_s$ by ~5–20 %.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Patient Impedance from Voltage and Current

For a real patient, the load is partly resistive and partly reactive. The analyzer measures peak voltage $V_{peak}$ and peak current $I_{peak}$ at pulse onset:

$$
Z = \frac{V_{peak}}{I_{peak}} \;\;[\Omega]
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Charge Time Estimate

The high-voltage charger delivers roughly constant current $I_{ch}$ into capacitor $C$ up to $V_{max}$. With charging efficiency $k$ (accounting for switching and thermal losses):

$$
t_{charge} \approx \frac{C \, V_{max} \, k}{I_{ch}}
$$

For a **typical AED-class defibrillator** ($C = 100\,\mu F$, $V_{max} = 1700\, V$, $I_{ch} = 1.5\, A$, $k = 1.3$):

$$
t_{charge} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1700 \cdot 1.3}{1.5} = \frac{0.221}{1.5} \approx 0.147\;\text{s}
$$

Wait — that's unrealistically fast. Let me re-check; charge current is closer to **0.3–0.5 A** in many designs:

$$
t_{charge} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1700 \cdot 1.3}{0.4} = \frac{0.221}{0.4} \approx 0.55\;\text{s}
$$

Still optimistic. Hospital defibrillators with $C = 32\,\mu F$, $V_{max} = 2000\, V$ typically need **6–7 s** because the charger is limited to protect battery life. The actual constant-power charging profile is:

$$
t_{charge} = \frac{E_s}{P_{ch}} \cdot k = \frac{360}{50} \cdot 1.3 = 9.36\;\text{s (battery limited)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Charge time depends on **battery state-of-charge**, **charger topology**, and **ambient temperature**. Test reports must specify all three.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Accuracy Tolerance (KTU Examiner's Favourite)

Suppose the analyzer reads $E_{measured}$ and the nominal set energy is $E_{set}$. The pass criterion per AAMI DF80 is:

$$
\frac{\lvert E_{measured} - E_{set}\rvert}{E_{set}} \times 100 \leq 15\%
\quad\text{AND}\quad
\lvert E_{measured} - E_{set}\rvert \leq 3\;\text{J}
$$

**Worked example:** $E_{set} = 200\,J$, $E_{measured} = 218\,J$.

$$
\Delta\% = \frac{218 - 200}{200} \times 100 = 9\%
$$

$$
\Delta J = \vert 218 - 200\vert = 18\;\text{J}
$$

Both must be ≤ 15 % AND ≤ 3 J. Here 18 J > 3 J → **FAIL**. The dual-condition clause (the "or 3 J, whichever is greater") actually **allows** up to 3 J of absolute deviation regardless of %. The correct interpretation per AAMI DF80 § 6.4 is:

> *The delivered energy shall be within ±15 % **or** ±3 J of the indicated value, **whichever is greater**.*

So 18 J deviation = 9 %, which is < 15 %. Therefore it **PASSES** the % test. The ±3 J clause only adds an extra tolerance floor for low energies. Re-evaluation:

- At 5 J setpoint: 15 % = 0.75 J, but ±3 J is greater → tolerance = ±3 J.
- At 200 J setpoint: 15 % = 30 J > 3 J → tolerance = ±30 J.

> **So 218 J (Δ = 9 %) PASSES** at the 200 J setting. KTU examiners love testing this nuance.

### 3.5 Analytical Test Matrix (Minimum Mandatory Tests)

| Test # | Test Parameter | Load (Ω) | Energy (J) | Pass Criterion | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy accuracy @ low | 50 | 2, 5, 10 | ±15 % or ±3 J | AAMI DF80 |
| 2 | Energy accuracy @ mid | 50 | 50, 100, 200 | ±15 % | AAMI DF80 |
| 3 | Energy accuracy @ high | 50 | 270, 300, 360 | ±15 % | AAMI DF80 |
| 4 | Energy accuracy @ high impedance | 100 | 200, 360 | ±15 % | AAMI DF80 |
| 5 | Energy accuracy @ low impedance | 25 | 200, 360 | ±15 % | AAMI DF80 |
| 6 | Charge time to 360 J (mains) | 50 | 360 | ≤ 7 s | IEC 60601-2-4 |
| 7 | Charge time to 360 J (battery) | 50 | 360 | ≤ 15 s | IEC 60601-2-4 |
| 8 | Synchronization delay | 50 | any | ≤ 60 ms | IEC 60601-2-4 |
| 9 | Sync-mode energy accuracy | 50 | 100, 200, 360 | ±15 % | AAMI DF80 |
| 10 | Leakage current, Earth (NC) | — | — | ≤ 0.5 mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| 11 | Patient leakage current (NC, CF) | — | — | ≤ 0.01 mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| 12 | Patient leakage current (SFC, CF) | — | — | ≤ 0.05 mA | IEC 60601-1 |
| 13 | Earth resistance (ground bond) | — | — | ≤ 0.1 Ω | IEC 62353 |
| 14 | Dielectric strength (mains) | — | — | 1500 V AC, 1 min | IEC 60601-1 |
| 15 | Discharge time after arming | 50 | 360 | ≤ 30 s internal bleed | IEC 60601-2-4 |
| 16 | Waveform tilt / duration | 50 | 200 | Per waveform spec | AAMI DF80 |
| 17 | Pacer output (if equipped) | 500 | — | ±10 % | IEC 60601-2-4 |
| 18 | SpO₂ / ECG / IBP self-test | — | — | Pass self-check | Mfr. spec |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Defibrillator Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains / Battery] --> B[High-Voltage Charger DC-DC]
    B --> C[HV Capacitor Bank 32-200 µF]
    C --> D[H-Bridge Switch Network IGBT]
    D --> E[Patient Load / Paddles]
    D --> F[Internal Test Load Bleed Resistor]
    E --> G[Defibrillator Analyzer]
    H[Microcontroller + UI] --> B
    H --> D
    I[ECG Front-End] --> H
    J[Sync R-wave Detector] --> D
    K[Event Memory + Printer] --> H
```

### 4.2 Defibrillator Analyzer Test Setup (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Visual Inspection Labels, Paddles, Cables] --> S2
    S2[Step 2: Earth Bond and Dielectric Test] --> S3
    S3[Step 3: Connect Analyzer Set Load 50 ohm] --> S4
    S4[Step 4: Charge at 2, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 270, 300, 360 J] --> S5
    S5[Step 5: Record Delivered Energy, Peak V, Peak I, Charge Time] --> S6
    S6[Step 6: Repeat at Loads 25, 75, 100, 150 ohm] --> S7
    S7[Step 7: Synchronized Cardioversion Test Verify delay ≤ 60 ms] --> S8
    S8[Step 8: Leakage Current NC and SFC Earth, Patient, Aux] --> S9
    S9[Step 9: Record Internal Discharge Time ≤ 30 s] --> S10
    S10[Step 10: Compile Test Report per IEC 62353] --> DONE[Sign and Archive]
```

### 4.3 Standard Test Report Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Header: Lab Accreditation, Standard References] --> R2
    R2[Section 1: Device Identification Make, Model, S/N, Firmware] --> R3
    R3[Section 2: Test Conditions Temp, Humidity, Mains, Battery] --> R4
    R4[Section 3: Reference Equipment Analyzer, Cal Date, Cert No] --> R5
    R5[Section 4: Visual Inspection Mechanical, Labels, Indicators] --> R6
    R6[Section 5: Electrical Safety Earth, Leakage, Dielectric] --> R7
    R7[Section 6: Performance Energy, Charge Time, Sync Delay] --> R8
    R8[Section 7: Waveform Analysis Scope Captures, Tilt, Duration] --> R9
    R9[Section 8: Pass/Fail Summary + Deviation Justification] --> R10
    R10[Section 9: Signatures Tester, Reviewer, Biomedical Engineer] --> DONE2[Report Issued]
```

### 4.4 Energy Discharge Through Different Loads (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[25 ohm: Higher current, Higher delivered energy] --> COMP[Compare to Setpoint]
    L2[50 ohm: Standard reference] --> COMP
    L3[100 ohm: Lower current, Lower delivered energy] --> COMP
    L4[150 ohm: Extreme adult impedance] --> COMP
    COMP[Pass if within ±15 percent or ±3 J]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** Define defibrillation and name the four waveforms commonly used in external defibrillators.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Defibrillation is the therapeutic delivery of a controlled DC shock to depolarize a critical mass of myocardium and terminate lethal arrhythmias such as ventricular fibrillation (VF) and pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT). The four waveforms are:
1. Monophasic Damped Sine (MDS)
2. Monophasic Truncated Exponential (MTE)
3. Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE)
4. Rectilinear Biphasic (RLB)

*(Any valid waveform family names – 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for naming four waveforms.)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** State the patient impedance range over which a defibrillator must deliver rated energy as per AAMI DF80.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Per AAMI DF80, the defibrillator must deliver the indicated energy within tolerance (typically ±15 % or ±3 J, whichever is greater) over a patient impedance range of **25 Ω to 200 Ω**, with **50 Ω** as the standard reference load for all energy measurements. The defibrillator analyzer must present one of the standard selectable loads (e.g., 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200 Ω) for compliance testing.

*(1 mark for range, 1 mark for 50 Ω reference, 1 mark for analyzer's selectable loads.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the functional subsystems of an external biphasic defibrillator. Discuss the role of the H-bridge switch network in delivering a biphasic waveform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A defibrillator uses a 100 µF capacitor charged to 1700 V. Compute (i) the stored energy, (ii) the energy delivered to a 50 Ω purely resistive load assuming 90 % discharge efficiency, and (iii) the peak current at the start of the pulse. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Block diagram and H-bridge explanation (7 marks):*

The biphasic defibrillator consists of six subsystems (1 mark each subsystem):

1. **High-voltage capacitor bank (C ≈ 32–200 µF)** — stores energy $E_s = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$.
2. **High-voltage charging circuit** — DC–DC flyback converter that charges the capacitor from the mains or internal Li-ion battery.
3. **H-bridge switch network** — four IGBT switches (S1, S2, S3, S4) arranged in an H configuration that steer current in either direction through the patient load.
4. **Paddles / adhesive pads** — patient interface with impedance-compensating gel.
5. **ECG acquisition + R-wave synchronizer** — enables synchronized cardioversion.
6. **Microcontroller, display, event recorder** — sets energy, sequences the discharge, logs events.

**Role of H-bridge (1 mark extra):**
During **phase 1**, switches S1 and S4 close while S2 and S3 stay open; current flows from +terminal through the patient to –terminal, producing a positive-polarity exponential decay. When the controller detects the target tilt (~60–70 %) or duration (~4–6 ms), S1, S4 open and S2, S3 close, **reversing the polarity** for phase 2 (typically 2–4 ms). This phase reversal removes residual membrane charge and lowers the defibrillation threshold.

**(b)** *Numerical solution (7 marks):*

Given: $C = 100\,\mu F = 100 \times 10^{-6}\, F$, $V_s = 1700\, V$, $R = 50\,\Omega$, $\eta = 0.90$.

**(i) Stored energy:**
$$
E_s = \frac{1}{2} C V_s^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times (1700)^2
$$
$$
E_s = 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2\,890\,000 = 144.5\;\text{J}
$$

**(ii) Delivered energy at 90 % efficiency:**
$$
E_d = \eta \times E_s = 0.90 \times 144.5 = 130.05\;\text{J}
$$

**(iii) Peak current:**
$$
I_{peak} = \frac{V_s}{R} = \frac{1700}{50} = 34\;\text{A}
$$

**Verification against IEC limit:** Peak voltage 1700 V < 5 kV → **PASS**. Delivered energy 130 J ≈ 144 J ± 15 % → **PASS**.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Describe the testing procedure of a defibrillator using a Defibrillator Analyzer as per AAMI DF80. List at least six mandatory test parameters with their pass criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a defibrillator PMT (Performance Verification Test), the following readings were obtained with a 50 Ω load: at 200 J setpoint the analyzer measured 178 J, at 360 J setpoint it measured 392 J, and the charge time from battery to 360 J was 12.4 s. Synchronization delay from R-peak to shock onset was 38 ms. Earth resistance was 0.08 Ω. Evaluate pass/fail for each test citing the relevant standard. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *AAMI DF80 Test Procedure (7 marks):*

The procedure follows these steps:

1. **Visual & mechanical inspection (0.5 mark):** Verify labeling (CE / ISO 13485), paddle integrity, cable insulation, indicator lamps, battery charge state, date/time, and self-test logs.
2. **Connect analyzer and select 50 Ω load (0.5 mark):** Standard reference load per AAMI DF80 § 6.4.
3. **Charge and discharge at each energy setting (1.5 marks):** Settings: 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 150, 200, 270, 300, 360 J. Record delivered energy, peak voltage, peak current, pulse width, and waveform tilt. Pass: ±15 % or ±3 J (whichever is greater).
4. **Repeat at loads 25, 75, 100, 150 Ω (1 mark):** Confirm tolerance holds over patient impedance range 25–200 Ω.
5. **Charge time measurement (1 mark):** From keypad press to "ready" indication. Pass: ≤ 7 s (mains) and ≤ 15 s (battery) for 360 J.
6. **Synchronization delay test (1 mark):** Inject ECG waveform with normal R-wave into defibrillator; trigger sync; measure delay. Pass: ≤ 60 ms.
7. **Internal discharge timer (0.5 mark):** After arming, leave for 30 s; verify automatic internal bleed. Pass: device should internally discharge.
8. **Leakage current and earth bond (0.5 mark):** Per IEC 60601-1 / IEC 62353. Earth resistance ≤ 0.2 Ω; CF patient leakage ≤ 0.01 mA (NC), ≤ 0.05 mA (SFC).
9. **Dielectric strength (0.5 mark):** 1500 V AC mains to earth for 1 minute. No breakdown.

**(b)** *Numerical pass/fail evaluation (7 marks):*

| Test | Setpoint | Reading | Deviation | Criterion | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy accuracy (low) | 200 J | 178 J | $\frac{200-178}{200}\times100 = 11\%$ | ±15 % | **PASS** |
| Energy accuracy (high) | 360 J | 392 J | $\frac{392-360}{360}\times100 = 8.89\%$ | ±15 % | **PASS** |
| Charge time (battery) | 360 J | 12.4 s | — | ≤ 15 s (IEC 60601-2-4) | **PASS** |
| Synchronization delay | — | 38 ms | — | ≤ 60 ms (IEC 60601-2-4) | **PASS** |
| Earth resistance | — | 0.08 Ω | — | ≤ 0.1 Ω (IEC 62353) | **PASS** |

**Final verdict: ALL TESTS PASS.** Defibrillator is approved for clinical use for the next 12 months (or per institutional policy). *(1 mark for table compilation and final verdict.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Misreading AAMI DF80 tolerance:** Many students write "±15 %" only, forgetting the "or ±3 J, whichever is greater" clause. At low energies (e.g., 2 J, 5 J), the 3 J clause dominates. **Cost: 1–2 marks per question.**
> 2. **Mixing up CF vs BF leakage limits:** CF applied part limit is **0.01 mA** (10 µA) NC; BF is 0.5 mA. Defibrillators must use **CF-type** applied parts. Mixing them up → **2 marks lost.**
> 3. **Confusing charge time limits:** 7 s is for mains-powered 360 J; 15 s is for battery. Writing 7 s for both → **1 mark lost.**
> 4. **Forgetting patient impedance range 25–200 Ω:** Test must be repeated across this range, not only at 50 Ω. Many students test only at 50 Ω → **3 marks lost.**
> 5. **Using $E = V \times I \times t$ instead of $E = \int v(t)^2/R\, dt$:** Energy delivered to a resistive load is voltage-squared dependent, not $V \cdot I \cdot t$. This is a 1-mark penalty per occurrence.
> 6. **Not distinguishing "stored" vs "delivered" energy:** Stored energy is what the capacitor holds; delivered energy is what reaches the patient. They differ by 5–30 %.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density):**

- **Defibrillator** = HV capacitor + H-bridge + paddles + controller. Stores energy $E_s = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$, delivers $E_d = \int v^2/R \, dt$.
- **Four waveforms:** MDS, MTE, BTE, RLB. Biphasic > monophasic clinically.
- **Energy tolerance:** ±15 % **or** ±3 J (whichever is greater) — per **AAMI DF80**.
- **Impedance range:** 25–200 Ω (adult), **50 Ω** standard test load.
- **Charge time:** ≤ 7 s (mains), ≤ 15 s (battery) for **360 J** — per **IEC 60601-2-4**.
- **Sync delay:** ≤ **60 ms** from R-peak to shock.
- **Leakage limits (CF applied part):** Patient leakage ≤ **0.01 mA** NC, ≤ **0.05 mA** SFC.
- **Earth resistance:** ≤ **0.1 Ω** — per **IEC 62353**.
- **Dielectric strength:** **1500 V AC**, **1 min** between mains and earth.
- **Analyzer selectable loads:** 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200 Ω.
- **Standards triad to memorize:** IEC 60601-1 (general), IEC 60601-2-4 (defib), AAMI DF80 (energy), IEC 62353 (recurrent test).
- **Patient applied-part class:** **Type CF** is mandatory (Cardiac Floating, defibrillation-proof).
- **Indicative energy:** Adult biphasic = 200 J → 300 J → 360 J; Monophasic = 360 J.
- **Pediatric dose:** **2–4 J/kg**.
- **Internal discharge:** Defibrillator must bleed internal energy within **30 s** if not delivered.
- **Test report sections:** Identification → Conditions → Reference Equipment → Visual → Electrical Safety → Performance → Waveform → Pass/Fail → Signatures.
- **NABL accreditation** required for third-party test labs in India.
- **Always sign and archive** reports for **minimum 5 years** per CDSCO / ISO 13485.
- **Energy is joules**, **voltage is volts**, **current is amps**, **impedance is ohms**, **charge time is seconds** — never interchange units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dissect and test Syringe and Infusion Pump using an analyzer to ensure they meet performance specifications, and generate a standard (IEC/ANSI/AAMI, etc.) safety, quality, and performance test report
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML607 - PCBML607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Dissect and test Syringe and Infusion Pump using an analyzer to ensure they meet performance specifications, and generate a standard (IEC/ANSI/AAMI, etc.) safety, quality, and performance test report

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Syringe Pump** is a small, positive-displacement, electromechanical medical device that uses a precision lead-screw / stepper-motor mechanism to drive the plunger of a disposable syringe, thereby delivering highly accurate, low-flow-rate (typically **0.1 mL/h to 1500 mL/h**) infusions of fluids, drugs, or nutrients. An **Infusion Pump** is a broader class of fluid-delivery device that may use peristaltic, cassette-based, elastomeric, or syringe mechanisms to deliver larger volumes (**1 mL/h to 1000 mL/h or more**) of intravenous fluids under positive pressure.

When the experiment states *"Dissect and test … using an analyzer"*, it refers to two parallel activities:
- **Dissection (Teardown Analysis):** Mechanical, electronic, and software decomposition of the device to identify sub-assemblies, bill-of-materials, and failure-prone sub-systems.
- **Analyzer-based Testing:** Use of a calibrated **Infusion Device Analyzer (IDA-4 / IDA-5 / Fluke Index 2 / Pronk Technologies SimSlim / Rigel UNI-SIM)** to record volumetric accuracy, occlusion pressure, trumpet-curve performance, and bolus release volumes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – Course Outcome (CO) Mapping:**
> This experiment is mapped to **CO3 (Apply standard test protocols to medical equipment)** and **CO4 (Generate IEC/ANSI/AAMI-compliant safety, quality and performance test reports)**. Students are expected to demonstrate competency in **L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)** levels of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – "The Precision Sauce Dispenser" 🍯**
> Imagine a chef who must pour exactly **2 mL of a rare spice** over a dish, every single time, for 1000 customers in a row. Pouring by hand would be a disaster. Instead, the chef attaches the spice bottle to a **motorised screw-threaded plunger** that pushes the bottle cap down by exactly one rotation per second. The chef reads the digital dial showing flow rate (**mL/h**) and total volume (**mL**). An alarm beeps if the bottle hits a jam (occlusion) or the battery dies.
>
> A **Syringe Pump** is *exactly* that screw-pusher – but for a patient's vein. An **Infusion Pump** is the same idea at a larger scale (think IV fluid bag instead of a spice jar). The **Analyzer** is a "smart cup with a weighing scale + pressure sensor" that catches what the pump delivers, weighs it to milligram precision, and plots how accurately the chef kept his promise.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used Throughout the Experiment

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** | Used for fluid head-pressure corrections |
| Water density at 20 °C $\rho$ | **998.2 kg/m³** | Volume ↔ mass conversion in analyzer |
| 1 mL of water at 20 °C | ≈ **0.9982 g** | Mass-based flow verification |
| Standard IV pressure (venous) | **5 – 15 mmHg** (~0.7 – 2 kPa) | Occlusion alarm threshold reference |
| Maximum allowable occlusion pressure (typical) | **300 – 750 mmHg** (40 – 100 kPa) | IEC 60601-2-24 limit |
| Mean flow-rate accuracy limit (IEC 60601-2-24) | **±5 %** | Mandatory pass/fail boundary |
| Trumpet-curve deviation (1 h) | **±5 %** | Mandatory for steady-state test |
| Battery backup (new standard) | **≥ 4 hours** at 25 mL/h | IEC 60601-2-24 § 51.101 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Syringe / Infusion Pump

The dissection reveals **five functional sub-systems**:

1. **Drive Sub-system** – Stepper/DC motor + lead screw + force sensor.
2. **Fluid Path Sub-system** – Syringe barrel, plunger, tubing, anti-free-flow clamp.
3. **Sensing Sub-system** – Pressure (upstream/downstream), bubble (air-in-line), position (encoder), temperature.
4. **Control Sub-system** – Microcontroller + firmware + non-volatile memory + watchdog timer.
5. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** – Keypad, LCD/LED, audio/visual alarms, RS-232 / USB / Wi-Fi port.

> [!TIP]
> **Why dissect?** Teardown lets us (a) identify counterfeit/substandard components, (b) verify IP-class seals (e.g., IPX4 splash-proofing), (c) locate the **defibrillation-proof CF-type applied part** symbol on the patient circuit (mandatory per IEC 60601-1).

## 2.2 The Performance Test Logic – Step-by-Step

The analyzer is connected in **series** with the pump's output. Flow of logic:

- **Step 1 – Warm-up & priming:** Run pump at 25 mL/h for 5 min to remove air bubbles.
- **Step 2 – Gravimetric calibration of analyzer:** Verify analyzer mass-reading with a Class-A reference weight (±0.1 %).
- **Step 3 – Set nominal flow rate:** e.g., 100 mL/h (low), 200 mL/h (medium), 400 mL/h (high).
- **Step 4 – Sampling window:** Analyzer records delivered mass at **Δt = 30 s** intervals.
- **Step 5 – Compute mean flow, % deviation, trumpet curve.**
- **Step 6 – Occlusion test:** Clamp downstream tubing; measure time-to-alarm and peak bolus volume on release.
- **Step 7 – Alarm verification:** Audio (≥ 65 dB at 1 m), visual, low-battery, end-of-infusion, KVO (Keep-Vein-Open).
- **Step 8 – Electrical safety per IEC 62353:** Earth-bond resistance, insulation resistance, earth / enclosure / patient leakage current.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Boundary / KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{V}{\Delta t}$ | mL/h | Use analyzer mean over last 2 h |
| 2 | Mass-based flow | $Q_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho \cdot \Delta t}$ | mL/h | $\rho$ = fluid density |
| 3 | % Accuracy (TruFlow) | $\%\text{Acc} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{meas} - Q_{set}}{Q_{set}} \times 100$ | % | Pass if $\vert\%\text{Acc}\vert \le 5$ % |
| 4 | Trumpet-curve window | $E_{n} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{n,\Delta t} - \bar Q_{n}}{\bar Q_{n}} \times 100$ | % | Plot vs observation window |
| 5 | Occlusion alarm pressure | $P_{occ} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{motor}}{A_{syringe}}$ | kPa | Typical 40–100 kPa |
| 6 | Pressure from head height | $P_{h} \;=\; \rho g h$ | Pa | h = height difference (m) |
| 7 | Bolus volume (Hooke analogy) | $V_{bolus} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{sys} \cdot \Delta P}{1}$ where $C_{sys}$ is tubing compliance | mL | Limit ≤ 1 mL (manufacturer) |
| 8 | Alarm sound pressure | $L_{p} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_{0}}\right)$ | dB | $p_{0} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 9 | Earth-bond resistance | $R_{e} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{test}}$ | Ω | ≤ 0.2 Ω per IEC 62353 |
| 10 | Patient leakage current | $I_{pat} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{m}}{R_{m}}$ | µA | ≤ 10 µA CF, ≤ 100 µA B |
| 11 | Battery endurance | $t_{bat} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{Ah} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{pump}}$ | h | ≥ 4 h at 25 mL/h |
| 12 | Trumpet-curve deviation | $D_{n} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{n}^{\max} - Q_{n}^{\min}}{\bar Q_{n}} \times 100$ | % | Must lie within ±5 % envelope |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** In the trumpet-curve plot, students often confuse the **observation window** ($\Delta t$) with the **trumpet-window**. The X-axis of the trumpet curve is the **observation window in minutes (5, 15, 30, 60, 120)**, not the elapsed test time.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospitals** use these tests in **incoming inspection, periodic maintenance (every 6–12 months) and post-repair** (per IEC 62353).
- **Manufacturers** (B-Braun, Becton-Dickinson, Baxter, Mindray) run the same test during **design verification (DV)** and **design validation (DV2)**.
- **Regulators (CDSCO, US-FDA, EU-MDR)** accept IEC 60601-2-24 as the **state-of-the-art** standard for infusion-device clearance.
- The **trumpet curve** is a single visual "fingerprint" that captures short-term variability, start-up transient, and steady-state drift – it is the *single most important* deliverable in the KTU experiment report.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mean Flow Rate and Trumpet-Curve Error

### 3.1.1 Mean Flow Rate from Discrete Mass Samples

The analyzer stores the cumulative delivered mass $m_{k}$ at the end of the $k^{th}$ sampling interval of duration $\Delta t$.

$$
\bar Q \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\,\sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac{m_{k}-m_{k-1}}{\rho\,\Delta t}
$$

The derivation:

- The volume delivered during the $k^{th}$ interval is
$$
V_{k} \;=\; \frac{m_{k}-m_{k-1}}{\rho}
$$
- The instantaneous flow rate at interval $k$ is
$$
Q_{k} \;=\; \frac{V_{k}}{\Delta t} \;=\; \frac{m_{k}-m_{k-1}}{\rho\,\Delta t}
$$
- The arithmetic mean over $N$ samples is then
$$
\bar Q \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}Q_{k}
$$
- Substituting and cancelling,
$$
\bar Q \;=\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\frac{m_{k}-m_{k-1}}{\rho\,\Delta t} \;=\; \frac{m_{N}-m_{0}}{N\,\rho\,\Delta t}
$$

This **last simplification** is *only* valid when $\Delta t$ is constant. Many analyzers (e.g., Fluke Index 2) hold $\Delta t = 30$ s exactly.

### 3.1.2 Trumpet Curve: Why We Compute It

The trumpet curve plots the **maximum and minimum percentage deviation** of the mean flow rate, computed over **observation windows** of increasing width $\Delta T_{n}$ (e.g., 5, 15, 30, 60, 120 min), as a function of $\Delta T_{n}$.

$$
D_{n}^{+} \;=\; \max_{i}\left\{\frac{Q_{i}(\Delta T_{n}) - \bar Q_{n}}{\bar Q_{n}}\right\}\times 100
$$

$$
D_{n}^{-} \;=\; \min_{i}\left\{\frac{Q_{i}(\Delta T_{n}) - \bar Q_{n}}{\bar Q_{n}}\right\}\times 100
$$

- The **upper envelope** $D_{n}^{+}$ (positive error) and **lower envelope** $D_{n}^{-}$ (negative error) form the two "trumpet bell" curves.
- A *narrow* trumpet at large $\Delta T_{n}$ indicates low long-term drift.
- A *wide* trumpet at small $\Delta T_{n}$ indicates pump-pulsation or sensor noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **The trumpet curve is the heart of IEC 60601-2-24 § 50.106 / 50.107.** Without it, the report is incomplete.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (Carry-Through for Exam)

**Given:** A syringe pump is set to deliver 100 mL/h. The analyzer logs the following delivered volumes over 4 consecutive 30-second windows during a 2-hour test:

| Window (k) | Delivered volume (mL) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 0.85 |
| 2 | 0.82 |
| 3 | 0.84 |
| 4 | 0.83 |

**Find:** (a) Mean flow rate, (b) % accuracy.

**Step 1 – Mean flow per window**

$$
\bar Q_{window} \;=\; \frac{0.85+0.82+0.84+0.83}{4} \;=\; 0.835\ \text{mL per 30 s}
$$

**Step 2 – Convert to mL/h**

$$
\bar Q \;=\; 0.835 \times \frac{3600}{30} \;=\; 0.835 \times 120 \;=\; 100.2\ \text{mL/h}
$$

**Step 3 – % Accuracy**

$$
\%\text{Acc} \;=\; \frac{100.2 - 100}{100}\times 100 \;=\; +0.2\%
$$

- The pump **passes** the ±5 % IEC limit.
- The expected trumpet width at the 2-minute window is ≈ ±0.6 % – a *very* healthy pump.

## 3.2 Occlusion-Detection Time and Bolus-Volume Analysis

### 3.2.1 Occlusion Alarm Time Derivation

When the downstream line is clamped, the motor continues to push. The pressure in the syringe barrel rises linearly with motor current $I$ until the force sensor threshold $F_{th}$ is crossed.

$$
P_{occ}(t) \;=\; \frac{F_{motor}(t)}{A_{syringe}} \;=\; \frac{k_t I(t) \cdot G_{mech}}{A_{syringe}}
$$

- $k_t$ = motor torque constant (Nm/A)
- $G_{mech}$ = mechanical gain of lead-screw
- $A_{syringe}$ = plunger cross-section (m²)

The alarm fires when

$$
P_{occ}(t_{alarm}) \;\ge\; P_{th}
$$

So

$$
t_{alarm} \;=\; \frac{P_{th}\,A_{syringe}}{k_t\,G_{mech}\,\dot I}
$$

where $\dot I$ is the rate of current ramp (A/s).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The examiner often tests whether you can state the *direction* of change: a **stiffer** tubing (larger $A$ or smaller $C_{sys}$) → *longer* alarm time; a **higher** pressure threshold → *longer* alarm time.

### 3.2.2 Bolus Volume on Occlusion Release

When the clamp is released, the elastic energy stored in the compliant tubing converts to a **bolus** (a sudden rush of fluid).

$$
V_{bolus} \;=\; C_{sys}\,\Delta P
$$

where $C_{sys}$ is the compliance of the entire patient-side circuit (typically 0.01–0.05 mL/kPa for adult IV sets).

**Example:** If $C_{sys} = 0.02$ mL/kPa and $\Delta P = 50$ kPa at alarm release,

$$
V_{bolus} \;=\; 0.02 \times 50 \;=\; 1.0\ \text{mL}
$$

- IEC 60601-2-24 § 50.108 generally requires that the **bolus volume be ≤ 1 mL** for an adult set, or **≤ 0.1 mL** for neonatal/ICU sets – *always check the manufacturer's specification in the IFU (Instructions For Use)*.

## 3.3 Electrical Safety (IEC 62353) – Leakage Current

For **Class-I, Type-CF** infusion pumps, the test sequence is:

1. **Earth-bond resistance** $R_{e} \le 0.2\ \Omega$ with **25 A** test current for ≥ 5 s.
2. **Insulation resistance** $R_{ins} \ge 200\ \text{M}\Omega$ at 500 V DC.
3. **Earth leakage current** $I_{earth} \le 500\ \mu\text{A}$ (normal condition, NC).
4. **Enclosure leakage current** $I_{enc} \le 100\ \mu\text{A}$ (NC), $\le 500\ \mu\text{A}$ (single-fault, SFC).
5. **Patient auxiliary current** $I_{pat} \le 10\ \mu\text{A}$ (CF, NC), $\le 50\ \mu\text{A}$ (CF, SFC).

The **current** measured by the safety analyzer is

$$
I \;=\; \frac{V_{m}}{R_{m}}
$$

where $R_{m}$ is the meter's input impedance (typically 1 kΩ for IEC 60601-1, or 10 kΩ as per some legacy methods). Modern IEC 62353 mandates the **MD (Measuring-Device) curve** with a frequency-compensated network.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Infusion Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains Input] --> B[SMPS Power Supply]
    B --> C[DC Bus 12V and 5V]
    B --> D[Battery Pack 11.1V Li-Ion]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Microcontroller Unit]
    C --> F[Stepper Motor Driver]
    F --> G[Stepper Motor]
    G --> H[Lead Screw Nut Assembly]
    H --> I[Plunger Drive Plate]
    I --> J[Syringe Barrel]
    J --> K[Patient IV Line]
    E --> L[Pressure Sensor Upstream]
    E --> M[Air In Line Sensor]
    E --> N[Encoder Position Sensor]
    L --> E
    M --> E
    N --> E
    E --> O[LCD Graphic Display]
    E --> P[Keypad Matrix]
    E --> Q[Audio Buzzer]
    E --> R[Alarm LED Array]
    E --> S[RS232 USB Ethernet]
    E --> T[Non Volatile Memory EEPROM]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Power flows top-down (A→C), control flows right (E→ everything), and feedback flows back to the MCU via dashed lines (L,M,N). This is the *exact* dissection wiring map you will encounter.

## 4.2 Test Setup Schematic – Infusion Pump + Analyzer + Safety Tester

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Infusion Pump Under Test] --> T1[Sterile IV Set]
    T1 --> A1[Flow Sensor Chamber of Analyzer]
    A1 --> C1[Collection Beaker on Load Cell]
    C1 --> A2[Mass Data Acquisition Module]
    A2 --> PC1[Laptop Running IDA Software]
    P1 --> S1[Electrical Safety Analyzer IEC 62353]
    S1 --> M1[Mains Receptacle Through Safety Tester]
    S1 --> PC1
    P1 --> AL1[Clamp For Occlusion Test]
    AL1 --> P1
    P1 --> P2[Programmer PC For Firmware Read Back]
```

## 4.3 Dissection Flow (Teardown Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Receive Pump in ESD Bag] --> S1[Photograph and Serial Log]
    S1 --> S2[Remove Enclosure Screws M3 x 8]
    S2 --> S3[Separate Front and Rear Housing]
    S3 --> S4[Disconnect Battery Connector First]
    S4 --> S5[Remove SMPS Module]
    S5 --> S6[Remove Main PCB with MCU]
    S6 --> S7[Remove Stepper Motor and Lead Screw]
    S7 --> S8[Remove Plunger Plate and Force Sensor]
    S8 --> S9[Inspect Tubing Path and Clamp]
    S9 --> S10[Label and Bag Each Sub Assembly]
    S10 --> S11[Bill of Materials Cross Check]
    S11 --> S12[Reassembly Reverse Sequence]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree – Pass / Fail of a Test Report

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{All electrical safety tests pass?}
    Q1 -- No --> FAIL1[Report FAIL - Safety Critical]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Flow accuracy within plus minus 5 percent?}
    Q2 -- No --> FAIL2[Report FAIL - Performance Critical]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Trumpet curve within envelope at 2 hour window?}
    Q3 -- No --> FAIL3[Report FAIL - Pulsation Issue]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Occlusion alarm pressure and time within spec?}
    Q4 -- No --> FAIL4[Report FAIL - Patient Safety Risk]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Battery backup at least 4 hours at 25 mL per hour?}
    Q5 -- No --> FAIL5[Report FAIL - Mobility Risk]
    Q5 -- Yes --> PASS[Report PASS - Issue Calibration Sticker]
```
