# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Define the "trumpet curve" in the context of infusion-pump performance testing. State the IEC standard that mandates it and the typical acceptance window.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The trumpet curve is a **graphical plot of the maximum and minimum percentage deviations of the mean flow rate** as a function of the observation window (ΔTₙ = 5, 15, 30, 60, 120 min). (1 mark)
- It is mandated by **IEC 60601-2-24 § 50.106–50.107** for infusion pumps and controllers. (1 mark)
- The acceptance window is **±5 % deviation** of the set flow rate, measured after the **2-hour** observation window following start-up. (1 mark)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**List any six internal sub-assemblies of a syringe pump that you would identify during dissection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, 0.5 each):**
1. **Stepper motor** with lead-screw drive mechanism.
2. **Plunger-driver plate** with force/pressure sensor.
3. **Control PCB** with microcontroller and EEPROM.
4. **HMI** – keypad, LCD, audio buzzer, LED indicators.
5. **SMPS power module** and **Li-Ion battery pack**.
6. **Sensors** – air-in-line, occlusion, encoder for plunger position.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer (14 Marks) – **Module Internal Choice**

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]*

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a syringe pump** as identified during dissection. List the function of **each block** and classify the components into **mechanical, electronic, and software sub-systems**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A syringe pump is set to deliver **200 mL/h** through a 50 mL disposable syringe. During a 2-hour gravimetric test, the analyzer (set to 30 s sampling) reports the cumulative delivered volumes at the end of every hour as: 199.2 mL, 199.7 mL, 200.4 mL, 200.1 mL. Calculate (i) the **mean flow rate**, (ii) the **percentage accuracy** at each hour, and (iii) state whether the pump **passes** the IEC 60601-2-24 limit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A

**(a) Block Diagram & Subsystem Classification (7 marks)**

*(Diagram – 3 marks; Subsystem classification – 2 marks; Function of each – 2 marks)*

The architecture of a syringe pump comprises the following five blocks:

| Block | Function | Sub-system |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Supply Unit (SMPS + Battery)** | Converts AC mains to regulated 5 V/12 V DC, charges the Li-Ion backup battery, isolates mains from the patient circuit. | **Electronic** |
| **Microcontroller (MCU) + Firmware** | Accepts user input, computes stepper pulses, monitors sensors, raises alarms, logs to EEPROM. | **Software + Electronic** |
| **Motor Driver (Stepper + Lead Screw)** | Translates electrical pulses to linear displacement of the plunger-driver plate. | **Electronic + Mechanical** |
| **Sensor Stack (Pressure, Air-in-Line, Encoder)** | Detects upstream occlusion, downstream blockage, air bubbles, and plunger position. | **Electronic + Mechanical** |
| **HMI (Keypad, LCD, Buzzer, LED)** | Allows the clinician to set rate/volume; provides audio-visual feedback and alarm signalling. | **Electronic + Software** |

**Mechanical sub-system** = lead screw, syringe cradle, plunger plate, force frame.
**Electronic sub-system** = SMPS, PCB, sensors, motor driver.
**Software sub-system** = firmware, alarm logic, trumpet-curve engine, watchdog.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given data:**

| Hour | Delivered volume (mL) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 199.2 |
| 2 | 199.7 |
| 3 | 200.4 |
| 4 | 200.1 |

**Set flow rate** = 200 mL/h; **Test duration** = 2 h (we are given 4 data points, presumably taken every 30 min – the question allows us to treat the 4 readings as cumulative end-of-half-hour marks).

**(i) Mean flow rate** (3 marks)

Step 1 – Take the average of the four cumulative readings:

$$
\bar V_{half} \;=\; \frac{199.2+199.7+200.4+200.1}{4} \;=\; \frac{799.4}{4} \;=\; 199.85\ \text{mL}
$$

Step 2 – Convert to mL/h (since each reading is over 30 min = 0.5 h):

$$
\bar Q \;=\; \frac{199.85}{0.5} \;=\; 399.7\ \text{mL/h}
$$

> **Note:** The 399.7 mL/h figure is the *apparent* mean. Because the set rate is 200 mL/h and the readings are 199–200 mL **per half-hour**, the **correct** per-hour flow is:

$$
Q_{i} \;=\; \frac{V_{i}\ \text{(per half h)}}{0.5} \;\Rightarrow\; Q_{1}=398.4,\ Q_{2}=399.4,\ Q_{3}=400.8,\ Q_{4}=400.2
$$

$$
\bar Q \;=\; \frac{398.4+399.4+400.8+400.2}{4} \;=\; \frac{1598.8}{4} \;=\; 399.7\ \text{mL/h}
$$

> **Correction / Examiner discretion:** If the question is interpreted as *per-hour* readings (cumulative at the end of every hour over a 4-hour test), then the mean per hour is exactly 199.85 mL/h, and the *set* rate of 200 mL/h is used directly. The expected KTU answer treats the data as **cumulative per half-hour over 2 h** with target 100 mL/h (per half-hour) – either interpretation is acceptable, but the student **must state the assumption**.

Assuming the second interpretation (more natural):

$$
\bar Q \;=\; 199.85\ \text{mL/h}
$$

**(ii) Percentage accuracy at each hour** (3 marks)

$$
\%\text{Acc}_{i} \;=\; \frac{Q_{i}-Q_{set}}{Q_{set}}\times 100
$$

$$
\%\text{Acc}_{1} \;=\; \frac{199.2-200}{200}\times 100 \;=\; -0.4\%
$$

$$
\%\text{Acc}_{2} \;=\; \frac{199.7-200}{200}\times 100 \;=\; -0.15\%
$$

$$
\%\text{Acc}_{3} \;=\; \frac{200.4-200}{200}\times 100 \;=\; +0.2\%
$$

$$
\%\text{Acc}_{4} \;=\; \frac{200.1-200}{200}\times 100 \;=\; +0.05\%
$$

**(iii) IEC pass/fail** (1 mark)

The maximum absolute deviation is **0.4 %**, which is well within the **±5 %** envelope of IEC 60601-2-24. **Result: PASS.** ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]*

**(a)** Describe the **safety, quality and performance tests** that must be conducted on an infusion pump per **IEC 60601-2-24** and **IEC 62353**. Prepare a **sample test-report template** in tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During an **occlusion test**, a syringe pump delivers 50 mL/h against a clamped line. The motor torque constant is $k_t = 0.05\ \text{Nm/A}$, the lead-screw gain is $G_{mech}= 4000$, the syringe plunger area is $A = 1.13\times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}$ (20 mL syringe), and the motor current ramps at $\dot I = 0.05\ \text{A/s}$. The alarm threshold is set to $P_{th}=80\ \text{kPa}$. Calculate (i) the **time to alarm**, and (ii) the **bolus volume** on release, given a tubing compliance $C_{sys}=0.02\ \text{mL/kPa}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B

**(a) Safety, Quality, Performance Tests (7 marks)**

**Quality tests** (per ISO 13485 / manufacturer QA plan):
- Visual & dimensional inspection, label legibility, IFU availability.
- Verification of serial numbers and calibration sticker.

**Electrical-safety tests** (per IEC 62353):
- Earth-bond resistance ≤ 0.2 Ω at 25 A.
- Insulation resistance ≥ 200 MΩ at 500 V DC.
- Earth leakage, enclosure leakage, patient auxiliary current per Class-I, Type-CF limits.

**Performance tests** (per IEC 60601-2-24):
- Volumetric flow-rate accuracy (low, medium, high).
- Trumpet-curve analysis at ΔTₙ = 5, 15, 30, 60, 120 min.
- Occlusion alarm pressure and detection time.
- Bolus volume on alarm release.
- Air-in-line alarm threshold.
- Battery endurance (≥ 4 h at 25 mL/h).
- Alarm sound pressure level (≥ 65 dB at 1 m).

**Sample test-report template (4 marks):**

| # | Test | Standard | Acceptance Limit | Observed Value | Pass/Fail |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earth-bond resistance | IEC 62353 | ≤ 0.2 Ω | 0.08 Ω | ✅ |
| 2 | Insulation resistance | IEC 62353 | ≥ 200 MΩ | 540 MΩ | ✅ |
| 3 | Earth leakage (NC) | IEC 62353 | ≤ 500 µA | 35 µA | ✅ |
| 4 | Enclosure leakage (SFC) | IEC 62353 | ≤ 500 µA | 78 µA | ✅ |
| 5 | Patient aux current (CF, SFC) | IEC 62353 | ≤ 50 µA | 8 µA | ✅ |
| 6 | Flow rate @ 25 mL/h | IEC 60601-2-24 | ±5 % | +1.2 % | ✅ |
| 7 | Flow rate @ 100 mL/h | IEC 60601-2-24 | ±5 % | –0.4 % | ✅ |
| 8 | Trumpet @ 2 h | IEC 60601-2-24 | ±5 % | ±0.8 % | ✅ |
| 9 | Occlusion alarm pressure | IEC 60601-2-24 | 40 – 100 kPa | 75 kPa | ✅ |
| 10 | Occlusion alarm time | IEC 60601-2-24 | ≤ manufacturer spec | 24 s | ✅ |
| 11 | Bolus on release | IEC 60601-2-24 | ≤ 1.0 mL | 0.6 mL | ✅ |
| 12 | Battery backup | IEC 60601-2-24 | ≥ 4 h at 25 mL/h | 5.2 h | ✅ |
| 13 | Alarm SPL | IEC 60601-1 | ≥ 65 dB(A) | 71 dB(A) | ✅ |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $k_t = 0.05\ \text{Nm/A}$
- $G_{mech} = 4000$
- $A = 1.13\times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}$
- $\dot I = 0.05\ \text{A/s}$
- $P_{th} = 80\ \text{kPa} = 80\,000\ \text{Pa}$

**(i) Time to alarm** (4 marks)

Formula:

$$
t_{alarm} \;=\; \frac{P_{th}\cdot A}{k_t \cdot G_{mech}\cdot \dot I}
$$

Substituting:

$$
t_{alarm} \;=\; \frac{80\,000 \times 1.13\times 10^{-4}}{0.05 \times 4000 \times 0.05}
$$

Numerator:

$$
80\,000 \times 1.13\times 10^{-4} \;=\; 9.04\ \text{N (force equivalent)}
$$

Denominator:

$$
0.05 \times 4000 \times 0.05 \;=\; 10\ \text{N/s}
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_{alarm} \;=\; \frac{9.04}{10} \;=\; 0.904\ \text{s} \;\approx\; 904\ \text{ms}
$$

**(ii) Bolus volume** (3 marks)

$$
V_{bolus} \;=\; C_{sys} \times P_{th} \;=\; 0.02\ \text{mL/kPa} \times 80\ \text{kPa} \;=\; 1.6\ \text{mL}
$$

> **⚠️ Result Analysis:** $V_{bolus}=1.6$ mL **exceeds** the typical 1.0 mL limit for adult sets. The pump **fails** the bolus-volume test – a clear teaching point that *the alarm may be fast, but the post-alarm bolus is the actual patient hazard.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Question Pitfalls – Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong standard citation** – Writing "IEC 60601" instead of "**IEC 60601-2-24**" for infusion-specific tests. The general standard is **-1**, the particular is **-2-24**. Always state the *particular* standard.
> 2. **Trumpet-curve X-axis confusion** – Plotting elapsed test time (min) instead of the **observation window** (5, 15, 30, 60, 120 min). The curve has *time-of-observation* on the X-axis, not total test time.
> 3. **Missing the bolus-volume pass/fail** – Computing $V_{bolus}$ is meaningless without comparing to the IFU/standard. A *correct numerical answer* with no pass/fail conclusion still loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting CF vs BF classification** – Infusion pumps that contact the heart or central circulation must be **Type CF (Cardiac Floating)**; this changes leakage limits by an order of magnitude.
> 5. **Skipping the photograph step** – In the dissection viva, students who cannot produce a *labelled* photograph of the disassembled view lose 1–2 marks instantly.
> 6. **Battery test at wrong flow rate** – The 4-hour endurance is specified at **25 mL/h** (low-rate worst case for battery). Testing at 400 mL/h is not valid evidence.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Syringe pump** = precision lead-screw + stepper, low flow (0.1–1500 mL/h), used in ICU/anaesthesia.
- [x] **Infusion pump** = broader class; volumetric, peristaltic, cassette, elastomeric.
- [x] **Dissection reveals 5 sub-systems**: Drive, Fluid path, Sensing, Control, HMI.
- [x] **Key test standards**: **IEC 60601-1** (general safety), **IEC 60601-2-24** (infusion specific), **IEC 62353** (routine electrical safety), **AAMI ID26**, **ANSI/AAMI HE75** (human factors).
- [x] **Mean flow accuracy** must be **±5 %** of set value.
- [x] **Trumpet curve** plots max/min % error vs observation window (5, 15, 30, 60, 120 min) – must stay within **±5 %** envelope at 2 h window.
- [x] **Occlusion alarm pressure** typically 40–100 kPa; alarm time depends on motor torque, lead-screw gain, current ramp, plunger area.
- [x] **Bolus on release** $V_{bolus} = C_{sys} \cdot \Delta P$ must be **≤ 1 mL** (adult) or **≤ 0.1 mL** (neonatal).
- [x] **Electrical safety** (IEC 62353): Earth bond ≤ 0.2 Ω; Insulation ≥ 200 MΩ; Patient aux current (CF, SFC) ≤ 50 µA.
- [x] **Battery endurance** ≥ **4 h at 25 mL/h**.
- [x] **Alarm SPL** ≥ **65 dB(A) at 1 m**.
- [x] **Mass ↔ Volume conversion** at 20 °C: 1 mL water ≈ 0.9982 g.
- [x] **Test report must include**: device ID, standards cited, ambient conditions (T, RH, BP), test engineer signature, pass/fail for *each* test, calibration-sticker number.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** in numerical answers (e.g., constant Δt, ambient temperature, sample orientation).
- [x] **Photograph and log** every dissection step; create a **bill of materials (BOM)**.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML607 - PCBML607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Perform dissection study on basic biomedical equipment (ECG machine/Automatic BP Machine)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dissection Study (Teardown Analysis)** in the context of biomedical engineering refers to the systematic, non-destructive physical and functional deconstruction of a medical device to identify its sub-assemblies, active and passive components, signal flow paths, and the engineering principle governing its operation. As per the **PCBML607 – Biomedical Instrumentation Laboratory** syllabus under the **2024 NEP-aligned Scheme**, dissection study of an **Electrocardiograph (ECG machine)** or an **Automatic Non-Invasive Blood Pressure (NIBP) monitor** involves opening the equipment enclosure, tracing the **block-level functional architecture** from sensor to display, identifying the role of each printed circuit board (PCB) stage, and correlating the physical hardware with the underlying physiological signal acquisition theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **ECG Machine**: An **electrocardiograph** is a bioelectrical signal recorder that captures the **electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)** — a representation of the electrical activity of the heart over time, obtained via surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest, typically within a bandwidth of **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** and amplitude range of **±0.5 mV to ±5 mV**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automatic BP Machine (NIBP Monitor)**: A non-invasive, automated **sphygmomanometer** that estimates **systolic, diastolic, and mean arterial pressure (MAP)** using the **oscillometric method**, employing an inflatable cuff, a pneumatic pump, a precision pressure transducer, and a microcontroller-based algorithm.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### ECG Machine – "The Heart's Electrical Microphone"
Imagine the heart as a powerful **loudspeaker** that plays a continuous electrical "rhythm." The ECG machine is essentially a **high-precision microphone and amplifier combo** that picks up these extremely faint electrical whispers (≈ **1 mV**) from the surface of the skin, filters out the loud "noise" of nearby electrical appliances (50/60 Hz mains hum, muscle tremors), and displays the amplified rhythm on a screen — like turning a barely-audible whisper into a clear concert recording.

### Automatic BP Machine – "The Tire Pressure Gauge with Intelligence"
Think of a digital tire-pressure gauge at a gas station: you press a button, an internal pump inflates the tire, a sensor measures the pressure, and a small chip displays the value. An automatic BP machine does the same thing, but instead of measuring air pressure in a tire, it measures **blood-pressure pulsations transmitted through a cuff wrapped around the upper arm** while the cuff is slowly deflated. The micro-controller "listens" to the oscillation amplitude in the cuff and mathematically derives systolic and diastolic values.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Benchmark Values)

| Parameter | ECG Machine | Automatic BP Machine |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Signal** | ±0.5 mV to ±5 mV | 0 to 300 mmHg (cuff pressure) |
| **Frequency Bandwidth** | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** | DC – 20 Hz (oscillation) |
| **CMRR** | **> 100 dB** | N/A |
| **Safety Class** | **Type CF, Class II** | **Type BF** |
| **Accuracy** | ±5 % (amplitude) | **±3 mmHg** (pressure) |
| **Standard Compliance** | IEC 60601-2-25 | IEC 80601-2-30 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: For the dissection study, students must identify the **right-leg drive (RLD) circuit**, **instrumentation amplifier (INA)**, **notch filter at 50 Hz**, **ADC resolution (≥ 10 bits, typically 12–24 bits)**, and the **defibrillation protection resistors** in the ECG front-end. For the NIBP monitor, the **piezoresistive pressure sensor**, **solenoid dump valve**, and the **oscillometric peak-envelope algorithm** are the focus components.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ECG Machine — Functional Block Decomposition

When you open the enclosure of a standard 3-lead or 5-lead ECG machine, you will typically encounter **five major functional blocks** arranged on separate (or integrated) PCBs:

### Block 1: Patient Interface & Protection Network
- **Electrodes** (Ag/AgCl) — convert ionic skin current into electronic current.
- **Patient Cable** — shielded to reject capacitive pickup; uses **defibrillation protection resistors** (typically **5 kΩ to 10 kΩ, 5 W**) and **gas-discharge tubes (GDT)** or **Transient Voltage Suppressors (TVS)** rated at **± 5 kV**.
- **ESD protection diodes** (e.g., BAV99) at the input.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters**: The patient must never receive a dangerous current. IEC 60601 stipulates a maximum **patient auxiliary current of 10 µA (CF)** under normal conditions and **50 µA** under single-fault conditions.

### Block 2: Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) Stage
The tiny ECG signal (≈ 1 mV) sits on top of a huge **DC electrode offset** (up to **± 300 mV**) and **common-mode noise** (50/60 Hz mains, several volts). A normal op-amp cannot do this job; we need an **Instrumentation Amplifier** built using **three op-amps** or a dedicated IC (e.g., **AD620, INA128, INA333**).

**Key equation for gain** (for a 3-op-amp INA):

$$G_{\text{INA}} \;=\; 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{\text{gain}}}$$

- $G_{\text{INA}}$ = Differential voltage gain (typically set to **10–100**)
- $R_1$ = Internal fixed resistor (e.g., **25 kΩ** for AD620)
- $R_{\text{gain}}$ = External gain-setting resistor

**Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):**

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \!\left( \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right)$$

- $A_d$ = Differential gain
- $A_{cm}$ = Common-mode gain
- **Target**: CMRR **> 100 dB at 50 Hz** for ECG.

### Block 3: Filtering Network
Three filter stages are mandatory:

1. **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** — cuts off DC and baseline wander. $f_c \approx$ **0.05 Hz** (sometimes 0.5 Hz for monitoring mode).
2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — removes high-frequency noise and anti-aliasing. $f_c \approx$ **150 Hz** (diagnostic) / **40 Hz** (monitoring).
3. **Notch Filter** — eliminates 50/60 Hz mains interference. Notch depth ≥ **40 dB**, Q ≈ **8–10**.

**Standard 2nd-order Sallen-Key LPF transfer function:**

$$H(s) = \dfrac{\omega_0^2}{s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}\,s + \omega_0^2}, \quad \omega_0 = 2\pi f_c$$

### Block 4: Right-Leg Drive (RLD) Circuit
The RLD is a **negative-feedback safety circuit**. The common-mode voltage sensed at the patient is inverted, amplified (gain ≈ **–100**), and fed back into the patient's right leg through a **current-limiting resistor (≈ 1 MΩ)**. This actively cancels common-mode interference and keeps the patient at a near-zero potential relative to the chassis.

### Block 5: ADC + Microcontroller + Display
- **ADC**: Typically **12-bit to 24-bit Sigma-Delta** (e.g., ADS129x series), sampling at **250 Hz to 1000 Hz** per channel.
- **Microcontroller**: ARM Cortex-M0/M4 or dedicated DSP.
- **Display**: LCD/TFT with **25 mm/s** sweep speed and **10 mm/mV** vertical calibration (standard ECG paper calibration).

## 2.2 Automatic BP Machine — Functional Block Decomposition

### Block 1: Pneumatic Subsystem
- **Cuff** (reusable nylon) — bladder size **12 × 22 cm** (adult), **6 × 11 cm** (neonate).
- **DC Pump** (diaphragm type) — inflates cuff to **180–220 mmHg**.
- **Solenoid Dump Valve** (or proportional valve) — controls deflation rate at **2–3 mmHg/s** (linear bleed).
- **Pressure Transducer** — piezoresistive Wheatstone bridge on a silicon diaphragm, range **0–300 mmHg**, output ≈ **5 µV/V/mmHg**.

### Block 2: Signal Conditioning for Pressure
- INA (e.g., AD620) amplifies the millivolt-level bridge output to **0–3.3 V** range.
- **Low-pass filter** at **20 Hz** (oscillations are slow).
- **16-bit ADC** samples pressure waveform at **100 Hz – 200 Hz**.

### Block 3: Oscillometric Algorithm (the "brain")
The pressure waveform during deflation is a **DC ramp (cuff pressure) with superimposed AC oscillations** (pulsations):

$$P_{\text{measured}}(t) = P_{\text{cuff}}(t) + A(t)\,\sin(\omega_p t)$$

- $P_{\text{cuff}}(t)$ = monotonically decreasing cuff pressure
- $A(t)$ = amplitude of the oscillation envelope (function of cuff pressure)
- $\omega_p$ = pulse frequency (≈ **1 Hz – 2 Hz**)

The algorithm:
1. Detects envelope peak amplitude $A_{\max}$ → at this cuff pressure, $P_{\text{cuff}}$ = **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)**.
2. **Systolic** = cuff pressure where $A(t)$ = **0.55 × $A_{\max}$** (on descending ramp).
3. **Diastolic** = cuff pressure where $A(t)$ = **0.85 × $A_{\max}$** (on descending ramp).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exact ratios (0.55, 0.85) are **empirical** and may differ slightly across manufacturers. The KTU examiner will accept the standard published ratios.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Quick-Reference)

| # | Concept | Formula | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | INA gain | $G = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}$ | $G \in [10, 100]$ |
| 2 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | **> 100 dB** |
| 3 | LPF cutoff (RC) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | 150 Hz |
| 4 | HPF cutoff (RC) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | 0.05 Hz |
| 5 | Notch frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | 50 Hz (India) |
| 6 | Patient safety current | $I_{\text{aux}}$ | **< 10 µA (CF)** |
| 7 | MAP (oscillometric) | $P_{\text{cuff}} \text{ at } A_{\max}$ | — |
| 8 | Systolic ratio | $A_{\text{sys}} = 0.55\,A_{\max}$ | Empirical |
| 9 | Diastolic ratio | $A_{\text{dia}} = 0.85\,A_{\max}$ | Empirical |
| 10 | ADC quantization | $V_{\text{LSB}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^n}$ | $n \ge 12$ bits |
| 11 | Defibrillation resistor | Limits $I$ to safe value | **5 kΩ – 10 kΩ** |
| 12 | ECG calibration | $10 \text{ mm/mV}$, $25 \text{ mm/s}$ | Standard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility**: ECG machines are used in ICUs, CCUs, stress-test labs, and Holter monitoring. NIBP monitors are integral to every multi-parameter patient monitor, ICU bed, ambulance, and home-health device. The dissection study bridges the **circuit theory** and **biomedical signal processing** classrooms with the **actual hardware** the student will service as a biomedical engineer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Output

We derive the closed-form differential gain of the standard **3-op-amp INA** used in the ECG front-end. The schematic has two input buffers (A1, A2) and a difference amplifier (A3).

**Step 1**: Apply the differential input $V_{\text{in}} = V_{in}^{+} - V_{in}^{-}$. Because the input op-amps have negative feedback, the voltage across the gain-setting resistor $R_g$ equals $V_{\text{in}}$.

$$V_{R_g} = V_{in}^{+} - V_{in}^{-} = V_{\text{in}}$$

**Step 2**: The current through $R_g$ is the same current flowing through the two feedback resistors $R_1$ (due to the virtual-open at the inverting inputs of A1, A2).

$$I_{R_g} = \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{R_g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{R_g}$$

**Step 3**: The differential voltage at the output of the first stage (between the outputs of A1 and A2) is:

$$V_{o1} = V_{oA1} - V_{oA2} = I_{R_g}\,(2R_1 + R_g) = V_{\text{in}}\!\left(\dfrac{2R_1}{R_g} + 1\right)$$

This is the gain of the first stage. Algebraic simplification:

$$V_{o1} = V_{\text{in}}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)$$

**Step 4**: The unity-gain difference amplifier (A3) rejects the common-mode and passes the differential. With $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$:

$$V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_2}\,(V_{oA2} - V_{oA1}) = V_{o1} \cdot 1 = V_{\text{in}}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)$$

**Step 5**: Final closed-form expression:

$$\boxed{\,G_{\text{INA}} = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\,}$$

**Worked numerical example** (AD620 with $R_1 = 24.7\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 1.1\;\text{k}\Omega$):

$$G_{\text{INA}} = 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 24{,}700}{1{,}100} = 1 + 44.91 \approx 45.9 \;(\approx 33.2 \text{ dB})$$

For a 1 mV ECG peak, the output is therefore **45.9 mV** — well within ADC range after the main amplifier.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 2nd-Order Sallen-Key Low-Pass Filter Cutoff

The standard Sallen-Key topology uses two capacitors $C_1$, $C_2$ and two resistors $R_1$, $R_2$ with a non-inverting op-amp buffer of gain $K = 1 + R_b/R_a$.

**Step 1**: Write the transfer function (unity-gain case, $K = 1$):

$$H(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 + s(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_1) + 1}$$

**Step 2**: Compare with the standard 2nd-order denominator:

$$s^2 + \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}\,s + \omega_0^2$$

**Step 3**: Equating coefficients:

$$\omega_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$

**Step 4**: Convert to Hz:

$$f_c = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$

**Step 5**: For equal-component design ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$):

$$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

**Worked numerical example** (designing a 150 Hz LPF):

Choose $C = 0.1\;\mu\text{F}$. Then:

$$R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 150 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 10.61\;\text{k}\Omega$$

A standard value of **10 kΩ** is used, giving $f_c \approx$ **159 Hz** (acceptable).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Oscillometric MAP, Systolic, Diastolic Mapping

Consider the recorded cuff-pressure signal $P(t)$. The algorithm performs these steps:

**Step 1 — Decompose signal**:
$$P(t) = \underbrace{P_{\text{cuff}}(t)}_{\text{slow ramp}} + \underbrace{p(t)}_{\text{oscillations}}$$

**Step 2 — Extract oscillation envelope**:
The AC-coupled component $p(t)$ is fed through a **peak detector** with a time constant of $\tau \approx$ **0.5 s** to give $A(t)$ (the envelope).

**Step 3 — Identify maximum envelope**:
$$A_{\max} = \max_t A(t) \quad \text{at} \quad t = t^*$$
$$\text{MAP} = P_{\text{cuff}}(t^*)$$

**Step 4 — Apply empirical ratios** (on the deflation phase, before $t^*$):
$$A(t_{\text{sys}}) = 0.55\,A_{\max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{\text{sys}} = P_{\text{cuff}}(t_{\text{sys}})$$

**Step 5 — Apply empirical ratio** (on the deflation phase, after $t^*$):
$$A(t_{\text{dia}}) = 0.85\,A_{\max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{\text{dia}} = P_{\text{cuff}}(t_{\text{dia}})$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check** (using standard physiological relationship):
$$\text{MAP} \approx P_{\text{dia}} + \dfrac{1}{3}(P_{\text{sys}} - P_{\text{dia}})$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component-to-Function Mapping (for Lab Record)

| Physical Component (Inside Equipment) | Function in Circuit/System | Test/Verification Method |
|---|---|---|
| Ag/AgCl snap electrodes | Biopotential pickup (ionic → electronic) | Visual + impedance check |
| TVS diodes / GDT | Defibrillation over-voltage protection | Apply ±5 kV pulse, check clamping |
| INA IC (AD620/INA128) | First-stage amplification (G ≈ 10–100) | Inject 1 mV, check output |
| Op-amp + RC network (HPF) | Blocks DC electrode offset (0.05 Hz) | Sweep function generator |
| Op-amp + RC network (LPF) | Anti-aliasing (150 Hz) | Sweep and check –3 dB point |
| Twin-T or active notch filter | 50 Hz mains rejection | Inject 50 Hz, check attenuation |
| RLD op-amp + 1 MΩ | Drives patient to ground potential | Measure common-mode voltage |
| 12–24-bit Σ-Δ ADC | Digitizes analog ECG | Logic analyzer on data lines |
| Microcontroller (ARM) | Runs filtering, HR calc, display | Trace firmware on oscilloscope |
| TFT/LCD module | Displays waveform + numerics | Apply test pattern |
| DC diaphragm pump | Inflates cuff to ~200 mmHg | Pressure gauge on hose |
| Solenoid valve | Bleeds air at 2–3 mmHg/s | Measure rate with stopwatch |
| Piezoresistive transducer | Converts pressure → voltage | Calibrate against manometer |
| INA for pressure | Amplifies bridge output | Inject known pressure |
| MCU (PIC/STM/Arduino-class) | Oscillometric algorithm | Trigger cycle, compare values |
| Battery + charger IC | Isolated power (Type CF safety) | Measure leakage current |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question] 
**List the major functional blocks of a 3-lead ECG machine and state the function of each in one line.**

**Model Answer**:
1. **Patient Interface & Protection Circuit** – electrodes + defibrillation resistors/TVS for safety.
2. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** – amplifies tiny differential ECG signal (≈ 1 mV) while rejecting huge common-mode noise.
3. **Filtering Network (HPF, LPF, Notch)** – removes DC offset, high-frequency noise, and 50 Hz mains interference.
4. **Right-Leg Drive (RLD) Circuit** – negative feedback loop to actively cancel common-mode voltage at the patient.
5. **ADC + Microcontroller + Display** – digitizes the cleaned signal, computes heart rate, and displays the waveform on the LCD.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question] 
**Explain the oscillometric principle used in an automatic BP machine with the help of the envelope amplitude–cuff pressure relationship.**

**Model Answer**:
The automatic BP machine uses the **oscillometric method**. An inflatable cuff is wrapped around the upper arm and inflated above the expected systolic pressure. As the cuff is slowly deflated (2–3 mmHg/s), the arterial pulsations create **small oscillations in the cuff pressure** waveform. The amplitude of these oscillations first increases, reaches a **maximum** (at **Mean Arterial Pressure, MAP**), and then decreases. 
The relationship is:
- **Systolic BP** ≈ cuff pressure when oscillation amplitude = **0.55 × maximum amplitude**.
- **Diastolic BP** ≈ cuff pressure when oscillation amplitude = **0.85 × maximum amplitude**.
- **MAP** ≈ cuff pressure at maximum oscillation amplitude.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **single-channel ECG machine**. Describe the function of the **instrumentation amplifier**, **right-leg drive circuit**, and **notch filter** in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-op-amp INA is built with $R_1 = 47\;\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_g = 2\;\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the differential gain. If the input ECG signal has a peak amplitude of **1.2 mV** with a common-mode interference of **2.0 V at 50 Hz**, and the INA's $A_{cm} = 0.001$, find (i) the differential output, (ii) the common-mode output, and (iii) the CMRR in dB.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of an **automatic non-invasive blood pressure (NIBP) monitor** using the **oscillometric method**. With a block diagram, show the role of the cuff, pump, transducer, microcontroller, and dump valve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In an NIBP cycle, the maximum oscillation amplitude is detected at a cuff pressure of **95 mmHg**. Using standard oscillometric ratios, determine the **systolic and diastolic BP**. Verify whether the standard physiological relation $\text{MAP} \approx P_{\text{dia}} + \tfrac{1}{3}(P_{\text{sys}}-P_{\text{dia}})$ is satisfied.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A:

**(a) Working of Single-Channel ECG Machine (7 Marks)**

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1**. The narrative:

The **patient electrodes** (Ag/AgCl) sense the bio-electric potentials from the body. These signals are extremely small (≈ **1 mV**) and are accompanied by a large DC electrode offset (up to ±300 mV) and 50 Hz mains interference. The **protection circuit** (defibrillation resistors, TVS, GDT) ensures patient safety against high-voltage transients.

The **INA** (e.g., AD620) provides high differential gain (≈ **10–100**) and a very high **CMRR (> 100 dB)**, which is essential to reject the common-mode noise. After the INA, the signal is processed by a **high-pass filter** ($f_c = 0.05$ Hz) to remove the DC offset, a **main amplifier** (gain ≈ **1000**) to bring the signal to the ADC's input range (≈ **1 V**), a **low-pass filter** ($f_c = 150$ Hz) for anti-aliasing, and a **50 Hz notch filter** to eliminate mains hum.

The **right-leg drive (RLD) circuit** senses the common-mode voltage at the patient (via averaging resistors from the two input leads) and drives an inverted, amplified copy of this voltage back into the patient's right leg through a **current-limiting resistor (≈ 1 MΩ)**. This active negative feedback **reduces the common-mode voltage to nearly zero** and improves the effective CMRR.

The cleaned, amplified analog signal is digitized by a **12–24-bit ADC** and processed by an **ARM microcontroller** that calculates heart rate, detects arrhythmias, and drives the **LCD display** at standard calibration (10 mm/mV, 25 mm/s).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the INA gain** using $R_1 = 47\;\text{k}\Omega$, $R_g = 2\;\text{k}\Omega$:

$$G = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g} = 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 47{,}000}{2{,}000} = 1 + 47 = 48$$

**Step 2 — Compute the differential output**:

$$V_{o,\text{diff}} = G \times V_{\text{in, diff}} = 48 \times 1.2\;\text{mV} = 57.6\;\text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the common-mode output**:

$$V_{o,\text{cm}} = A_{cm} \times V_{\text{in, cm}} = 0.001 \times 2.0\;\text{V} = 2.0\;\text{mV}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the CMRR in dB**:

$$\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{G}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{48}{0.001}\right) = 20 \times \log_{10}(48{,}000)$$

$$= 20 \times 4.681 = \mathbf{93.6\;\text{dB}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall**:
> - **Do not** confuse $A_{cm}$ (common-mode gain, a small number like 0.001) with the **CMRR** itself. The two are related by $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$.
> - **Do not** forget to apply the factor of $G$ when computing $V_{o,\text{diff}}$; many students write $V_{o,\text{diff}} = V_{\text{in, diff}}$ which is wrong.
> - **Unit mismatch**: $V_{\text{in, cm}}$ is in **V** while $V_{\text{in, diff}}$ is in **mV** — keep units consistent or convert both to volts first.

---

### Solution to Question B:

**(a) NIBP Monitor – Construction & Working (7 Marks)**

Refer to **Section 4.2** for the block diagram. The narrative:

An NIBP monitor consists of a **pneumatic subsystem** and an **electronic subsystem**. The **cuff** (nylon wrap-around with an internal rubber bladder) is wrapped around the upper arm at heart level. The **DC diaphragm pump** (driven by a MOSFET switch under MCU control) inflates the cuff to a target pressure of typically **180–220 mmHg**, well above the expected systolic pressure. The **pressure transducer** (a piezoresistive Wheatstone bridge on a silicon diaphragm) outputs a millivolt-level analog voltage proportional to the instantaneous cuff pressure.

The transducer signal is amplified by an **INA**, low-pass filtered at **≈ 20 Hz** (since arterial pulsations are slow), and digitized by a **16-bit ADC**. The **microcontroller** (running the oscillometric algorithm) identifies the **envelope of the pulsation amplitudes** during slow cuff deflation (controlled by the **solenoid dump valve** at ≈ **2–3 mmHg/s**). It then determines **MAP** (at maximum envelope amplitude), **systolic** (at 0.55 × max), and **diastolic** (at 0.85 × max), and displays them on the LCD along with pulse rate.

**Safety features**: automatic over-pressure limit (typically **300 mmHg**), automatic cuff deflation on power failure, and **Type BF isolation** between patient and mains.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given**: $A_{\max}$ occurs at cuff pressure = **95 mmHg** → so **MAP = 95 mmHg**.

**Step 1 — Systolic BP** (using 0.55 ratio):
The cuff pressure is **higher** than 95 mmHg when the amplitude is rising to $A_{\max}$. The 0.55 ratio is found on the **rising edge** of the envelope. To find the corresponding cuff pressure, we need the envelope function, which the problem does not provide. The standard oscillometric approximation states that:

> **MAP is the cuff pressure at $A_{\max}$**, and the standard empirical relationship holds:
> $$\text{MAP} = P_{\text{dia}} + \tfrac{1}{3}(P_{\text{sys}} - P_{\text{dia}})$$

**Step 2 — Diastolic BP** (using 0.85 ratio, on the falling edge): similarly requires envelope. We use the assumed realistic clinical value here. For the KTU numerical, we are given that the algorithm found:

**Given output of algorithm**: $P_{\text{sys}} = 120$ mmHg, $P_{\text{dia}} = 80$ mmHg (this is the expected output the student should state based on the empirical relationships from a typical published envelope curve, as the problem does not provide the envelope shape).

**Step 3 — Verify the MAP relationship**:

$$\text{MAP}_{\text{calc}} = P_{\text{dia}} + \tfrac{1}{3}(P_{\text{sys}} - P_{\text{dia}}) = 80 + \tfrac{1}{3}(120 - 80)$$

$$= 80 + \tfrac{1}{3}(40) = 80 + 13.33 = 93.33\;\text{mmHg}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with measured MAP = 95 mmHg**:

$$|\text{Error}| = \left|\dfrac{95 - 93.33}{95}\right| \times 100\% \approx 1.76\%$$

This is well within the **±3 mmHg accuracy** specification of a clinical-grade NIBP monitor. ✅ **Relation verified.**

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**:
> 1. **Confusing INA gain and total gain** — the INA is only the *first* stage. Total ECG gain = $G_{\text{INA}} \times G_{\text{main-amp}}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the 20 × log factor** in CMRR — students often write "CMRR = 93.6" instead of "93.6 dB".
> 3. **Stating "0.55" and "0.85" without context** — always say: *"Systolic = 0.55 × $A_{\max}$ on the rising envelope"* and *"Diastolic = 0.85 × $A_{\max}$ on the falling envelope"*.
> 4. **Ignoring patient safety standards** — never omit the IEC 60601 classification (**Type CF for ECG, Type BF for NIBP**) in the dissection description.
> 5. **Not labelling components with their part numbers** — saying "op-amp" instead of "INA — AD620" loses at least 1 mark in a 14-mark question.
> 6. **Mixing up the two machine types** — the ECG measures *electrical* signals (mV), while the NIBP measures *mechanical* signals (mmHg via pressure → voltage).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ECG Machine — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] ECG measures **electrical activity of the heart** (P, QRS, T waves), signal ≈ **1 mV**, bandwidth **0.05–150 Hz**.
- [ ] Front-end is an **INA** (e.g., **AD620 / INA128 / INA333**), gain typically **10–100**, CMRR **> 100 dB**.
- [ ] **INA gain formula**: $G = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}$.
- [ ] **Three filters** are mandatory: **HPF (0.05 Hz), LPF (150 Hz), Notch (50 Hz)**.
- [ ] **Right-Leg Drive (RLD)** is an active feedback loop that reduces common-mode voltage; it uses a **1 MΩ** current-limiting resistor for safety.
- [ ] Patient protection uses **5 kΩ – 10 kΩ defibrillation resistors** and **TVS / GDT** components.
- [ ] ADC is typically **12–24 bit Sigma-Delta** sampling at **≥ 250 Hz**; calibration is **10 mm/mV** vertical, **25 mm/s** horizontal.
- [ ] **Type CF, Class II** isolation per **IEC 60601-2-25**; patient leakage current **< 10 µA**.
- [ ] Five dissection blocks: **Patient Interface → INA → Filter Bank → RLD → ADC + MCU + Display**.

### Automatic BP Machine — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] NIBP uses the **oscillometric method** — non-invasive, automated.
- [ ] Components: **Cuff + DC Pump + Pressure Transducer + Solenoid Valve + MCU + Display**.
- [ ] Cuff inflates to **180–220 mmHg**; deflated at **2–3 mmHg/s** by a **solenoid dump valve**.
- [ ] **Pressure transducer**: piezoresistive Wheatstone bridge, range **0–300 mmHg**, output **≈ 5 µV/V/mmHg**.
- [ ] The pressure signal has a **DC ramp + AC oscillations** — the AC envelope is what carries BP information.
- [ ] **MAP = cuff pressure at maximum oscillation amplitude** ($A_{\max}$).
- [ ] **Systolic = 0.55 × $A_{\max}$** (rising edge), **Diastolic = 0.85 × $A_{\max}$** (falling edge).
- [ ] Cross-check: $\text{MAP} = P_{\text{dia}} + \tfrac{1}{3}(P_{\text{sys}} - P_{\text{dia}})$.
- [ ] **Type BF** isolation per **IEC 80601-2-30**; accuracy **±3 mmHg**.
- [ ] Safety: over-pressure limit at **≈ 300 mmHg**, auto-deflate on power fail.

### Universal Lab-Record Tips (for full marks)

- [ ] **Always draw the block diagram first** before writing theory.
- [ ] **Label every block with the actual IC part number** (AD620, LM358, etc., as observed during dissection).
- [ ] **Mention signal levels (mV / V) at each stage** — this is what differentiates a top-scoring answer from an average one.
- [ ] **Quote the relevant IEC safety standard** for the device.
- [ ] **State the standard calibration values** (10 mm/mV, 25 mm/s for ECG).
- [ ] **Conclude with a short paragraph on patient safety and isolation** — this is a KTU examiner favourite.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Defibrillator Dissection Study — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML607 - PCBML607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Perform dissection study on defibrillator

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **defibrillator** is a life-saving electromedical device designed to deliver a therapeutic dose of electrical energy to the heart of a patient suffering from life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias such as **ventricular fibrillation (VF)** or **pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT)**. The procedure of **dissection study** involves the systematic physical teardown and functional analysis of the defibrillator's internal architecture to understand its **high-voltage energy storage**, **synchronization circuitry**, **waveform shaping networks**, **patient isolation barriers**, and **biopotential monitoring subsystems**.

As per the KTU 2024 B.Tech (Biomedical Engineering) practical curriculum, a dissection study aims to:
- Identify and label every internal hardware block.
- Trace the signal/power flow from AC mains → DC supply → High-Voltage Converter → Storage Capacitor → Discharge Network → Patient Electrodes.
- Measure key circuit parameters using standard test equipment.
- Correlate physical components with the corresponding schematic block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCBML607 / Module 2):** The dissection study bridges *theoretical circuit knowledge* with *practical hardware realization* of a therapeutic device. Students are expected to draw a fully labelled block diagram, photograph the PCBs/sub-assemblies, and present a structured technical report following the KTU laboratory record format.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Defibrillator as a Controlled Lightning Cannon"

Imagine a **fire-extinguisher-sized lightning cannon** sitting in a hospital crash-cart:

1. **Charging Phase:** A small battery (12 V) drives a high-voltage DC-DC converter that slowly pumps electrons into a giant "water tank" (the energy-storage capacitor) — just like filling a pressurized water tank from a hand-pump.
2. **Pressurization:** Once the tank is full, the pressure (voltage) is so high (≈ 1500–3600 V) that it would be lethal if released carelessly.
3. **Discharge Phase:** A precisely controlled **valve** (the H-bridge / IGBT switch) releases a measured burst through the patient's chest via two **paddles** (electrodes).
4. **Effect:** The synchronized electrical "shock" momentarily *stuns* the chaotic heart muscle, allowing the heart's natural pacemaker (the SA node) to resume a normal sinus rhythm.

> [!NOTE]
> The **heart is a self-firing electrical organ**. During fibrillation, the electrical activity becomes disorganized, like a room full of people shouting at once. A defibrillator does NOT "restart" the heart — it *resets* the electrical activity by depolarizing the entire myocardium simultaneously, after which the natural pacemaker takes over.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Remember)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Energy range (adult) | **2 J to 360 J** | Energy stored in capacitor |
| Energy range (pediatric) | **2 J to 100 J** | Lower doses for children |
| Storage capacitance | **32 μF to 200 μF** | Most common: 32 μF, 50 μF |
| Peak voltage | **1500 V to 3600 V** | Depends on selected energy |
| Transthoracic impedance (TTI) | **25 Ω to 150 Ω** | Treated as ≈ 50 Ω standard |
| Discharge time | **4 ms to 20 ms** | Biphasic: 4–12 ms; Monophasic: 12–20 ms |
| Charge time (to 360 J) | **< 7 seconds** | IEC 60601-2-4 requirement |
| Mains input | **220–240 V, 50 Hz** | Indian KTU standard |

## 1.4 Pre-Dissection Safety Protocol

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pre-Lab Mandate:** Before opening the defibrillator chassis, the examiner will verify that you have followed safety protocol. Loss of 2–3 marks is common for skipping this step.

1. **Verify the device is OFF** and disconnected from mains.
2. **Discharge all capacitors** using a 10 kΩ / 25 W bleeder resistor across the high-voltage terminals — wait **at least 5 minutes**.
3. Verify zero residual voltage with a **high-impedance (>10 GΩ) HV probe** or **kilovoltmeter**.
4. Wear **insulated ESD-safe gloves** and use **one-hand rule** when probing live circuits.
5. Ensure the workbench has an **isolated transformer** and **RCD (residual-current device)**.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Architecture of a Generic Defibrillator

A modern defibrillator is organized into **six functional blocks**:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  AC MAINS / BATTERY → DC SUPPLY (SMPS)              │
│        ↓                                             │
│  HIGH-VOLTAGE DC-DC CONVERTER (Inverter)             │
│        ↓                                             │
│  ENERGY STORAGE CAPACITOR (C)                        │
│        ↓                                             │
│  CHARGE / DISCHARGE CONTROL (IGBT/SCR + Driver)      │
│        ↓                                             │
│  WAVEFORM SHAPING NETWORK (Inductor, Diodes)         │
│        ↓                                             │
│  PATIENT INTERFACE (Paddles / Pads + Isolation)      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.1.1 Block-by-Block Functional Logic

**Block 1 — Low-Voltage DC Supply**
- A switch-mode power supply (SMPS) converts 220 V AC to multiple low-voltage DC rails (+5 V, +12 V, +15 V, –15 V) for the control electronics, microprocessor, LCD/LED indicators, and front-panel keypad.
- Battery backup: A sealed lead-acid (SLA) battery, typically **12 V / 4 Ah**, allows portable operation.

**Block 2 — High-Voltage DC-DC Converter (HV Inverter)**
- A low-voltage DC (≈ 12 V) is chopped by a high-frequency oscillator, fed into a step-up transformer, and rectified to produce the high DC voltage required to charge the storage capacitor.
- Topology: Often a **flyback** or **forward converter** with a turns ratio of 1:60 to 1:200.
- Output voltage range: 0 to 3700 V DC.

**Block 3 — Energy Storage Capacitor (ESC)**
- The heart of the defibrillator. Stores electrical energy as a high electric field across a thin dielectric.
- Dielectric material: **metallized polypropylene film** (self-healing) or **aluminum electrolytic** in older units.
- Typical values: **32 μF / 3600 V** or **50 μF / 2000 V**.

**Block 4 — Charge / Discharge Control Switches**
- **Charging:** A high-voltage diode (e.g., HV-D1) isolates the capacitor from the converter.
- **Discharging:** A solid-state switch — **IGBT, SCR (thyristor), or MOSFET** — connects the capacitor to the waveform-shaping network on command from the operator or the synchronization circuit.

**Block 5 — Waveform Shaping Network (WSN)**
- For **monophasic damped sinusoidal (MDS):** A series inductor (L ≈ 25–50 mH) and a parallel resistor shape the exponential-decay discharge.
- For **biphasic truncated exponential (BTE):** An **H-bridge** of four switches reverses the polarity of the second phase after a fixed duration.

**Block 6 — Patient Interface**
- **Paddles (hard):** Reusable metal plates with insulating handles, used with conductive gel.
- **Pads (soft):** Self-adhesive hydrogel pads for AED / pre-hospital use.
- **Isolation barrier:** Optical isolators or isolation transformers (≥ 5000 V dielectric withstand) for ECG input.
- **Defibrillator protection:** A series of high-power diodes/TVS (transient voltage suppressors) protects the ECG amplifier from the discharge voltage.

---

## 2.2 Operating Modes of a Defibrillator

| Mode | Function | Sync | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual** | Operator selects energy and timing | No | By trained personnel |
| **Synchronized (Cardioversion)** | Shock delivered at the R-wave peak | **Yes** | Atrial fibrillation, PSVT, stable VT |
| **Automated External Defibrillator (AED)** | Algorithm detects shockable rhythm | Auto | Lay rescuer / public access |
| **Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD)** | Internal, automatic | Auto | High-risk patients |

> [!NOTE]
> The **synchronization circuit** ensures the shock does NOT fall on the T-wave (vulnerable period of repolarization), which could otherwise convert a stable tachycardia into fatal ventricular fibrillation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | **Energy stored in capacitor** | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E}{C}}$ | **Voltage for desired energy** | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $I_{peak} = \dfrac{V}{R_{TTI}}$ | **Initial discharge current** | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $E_{delivered} = I_{peak}^2 \cdot R_{TTI} \cdot t_{eff}$ | **Energy delivered to patient** | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | **RC time constant (monophasic)** | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | **Monophasic exponential decay** | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $I(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{R} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | **Monophasic current decay** | Amperes (A) |
| 8 | $E(t) = E_0 \cdot e^{-2t/\tau}$ | **Energy decay over time** | Joules (J) |
| 9 | $t_{crit} = \tau \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_f}\right)$ | **Time to drop from E₀ to E_f** | seconds (s) |
| 10 | $V_{out(rms)} = N \cdot \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | **Transformer EMF equation** | Volts (V) |
| 11 | $f_{osc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | **HV converter switching freq.** | Hertz (Hz) |
| 12 | $P_{out} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | **Average charging power** | Watts (W) |
| 13 | $Z_{TTI} = \sqrt{R^2 + X_c^2}$ | **Transthoracic impedance** | Ohms (Ω) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam answers, always state the **assumed standard load** of **50 Ω** for transthoracic impedance when not specified by the problem. This is the IEC-standard test load.

## 2.4 Energy Waveform Mathematics (Detailed)

### 2.4.1 Monophasic Damped Sinusoidal (MDS)

The capacitor discharges through an R-L-C loop. The governing equation is:

$$L\dfrac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R\dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{i}{C} = 0$$

Depending on the discriminant $R^2 - 4L/C$:

- **Overdamped** ($R^2 > 4L/C$): no oscillation — *purely exponential*
- **Critically damped** ($R^2 = 4L/C$): fastest non-oscillatory decay
- **Underdamped** ($R^2 < 4L/C$): damped sinusoid (the classic "Lown waveform")

The damped sinusoidal current is:

$$i(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha t} \cdot \sin(\omega_d t)$$

where

$$\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}, \qquad \omega_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{LC} - \alpha^2}$$

### 2.4.2 Biphasic Truncated Exponential (BTE)

A biphasic waveform delivers two phases:
- **Phase 1 (positive):** Duration $t_1$ ≈ 4–6 ms
- **Phase 2 (negative):** Duration $t_2$ ≈ 4–6 ms
- **Interphase pause:** ≈ 0.2 ms

Energy in each phase is:

$$E_1 = \int_0^{t_1} \dfrac{V_1^2(t)}{R_{TTI}} dt, \qquad E_2 = \int_{t_1}^{t_1+t_2} \dfrac{V_2^2(t)}{R_{TTI}} dt$$

The **second phase** is often truncated when the trailing edge voltage drops to a fixed ratio (typically 50% of the initial phase-2 voltage).

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stored Energy in the Defibrillator Capacitor

**Problem statement:** A defibrillator uses a 32 μF capacitor. What voltage must it be charged to in order to deliver exactly 360 J?

### Step 1 — Write the fundamental energy-storage equation

The energy stored in a capacitor with capacitance $C$ charged to voltage $V$ is the integral of instantaneous power over time:

$$E = \int_0^{Q} V \, dq = \int_0^{Q} \dfrac{q}{C} \, dq = \dfrac{1}{C} \cdot \dfrac{Q^2}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Express in terms of voltage

Substituting $Q = C \cdot V$:

$$E = \dfrac{1}{C} \cdot \dfrac{(C \cdot V)^2}{2} = \dfrac{C^2 V^2}{2C} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$$

> This is the **defining equation of the defibrillator's energy budget**.

### Step 3 — Solve for V (the required charging voltage)

Rearranging the energy equation for $V$:

$$V^2 = \dfrac{2E}{C} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E}{C}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values

$$E = 360 \text{ J}, \qquad C = 32 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 360}{32 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{720}{32 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$V = \sqrt{22.5 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$V \approx 4743 \text{ V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Real commercial defibrillators cap the voltage at **≈ 3600 V** for safety. To deliver 360 J at 3600 V, the required capacitance is:

$$C = \dfrac{2E}{V^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 360}{3600^2} = 5.56 \times 10^{-5} \text{ F} = 55.6 \text{ μF}$$

This is why the most common capacitor values are **32 μF** and **50 μF**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Peak Discharge Current Through Patient

**Problem statement:** A fully charged 32 μF / 3600 V defibrillator discharges through a 50 Ω transthoracic impedance. Find the peak current.

### Step 1 — Apply Ohm's law at t = 0 (capacitor voltage = maximum)

$$I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_0}{R_{TTI}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$I_{peak} = \dfrac{3600}{50} = 72 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the actual energy delivered (energy delivered is LESS than stored because of capacitor charge retention)

For an exponential decay through R-C:

$$E_{delivered} = E_{stored} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-2T/\tau}\right)$$

where $T$ is the total discharge time and $\tau = R \cdot C = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \text{ ms}$.

Assuming $T = 4\tau$ (a typical truncation point):

$$E_{delivered} = 360 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-8}\right) \approx 360 \cdot 0.9997 \approx 359.9 \text{ J}$$

> Hence, almost all stored energy is delivered in 4 time-constants (≈ 6.4 ms).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Charge Time of the Energy Storage Capacitor

**Problem statement:** A defibrillator's HV converter delivers an average charging current of 1.5 A at 1500 V. How long does it take to charge a 32 μF capacitor to 3600 V?

### Step 1 — Energy balance

The energy to be stored is $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = 360$ J.

The average power delivered by the converter is:

$$P = V \cdot I = 1500 \times 1.5 = 2250 \text{ W}$$

### Step 2 — Ideal (100% efficient) charge time

$$t_{charge} = \dfrac{E}{P} = \dfrac{360}{2250} = 0.16 \text{ s}$$

> Real charge time (with ≈ 60–70% converter efficiency) ≈ **3–7 seconds**, matching the IEC specification.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Synchronization Delay & R-Wave Detection

**Problem statement:** The R-wave detector of a synchronized cardioverter introduces a 30 ms delay. The R-wave is detected at the 90% peak of the QRS. Determine the synchronization margin to the vulnerable T-wave.

### Step 1 — Heart rate assumption

Assume a normal sinus rhythm of 75 bpm. The R-R interval is:

$$T_{RR} = \dfrac{60}{75} = 0.8 \text{ s} = 800 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2 — Vulnerable period

The T-wave begins roughly 280 ms after the QRS and the vulnerable window is ≈ 30 ms long.

### Step 3 — Synchronization margin

Shock delivered at $T_{delay} = 30$ ms after the R-peak.

$$T_{margin} = 280 - 30 = 250 \text{ ms}$$

> The shock lands well within the safe **refractory period** of the ventricles.

---

## 3.6 Sample Numerical Problem (Full Solution)

**Problem:** A monophasic defibrillator has a 50 μF capacitor charged to 2000 V. It discharges through a 50 Ω load. Calculate: (i) energy stored, (ii) peak current, (iii) RC time constant, (iv) voltage across load after 5 ms, (v) energy delivered in the first 5 ms.

### Solution

**(i) Energy stored:**

$$E_0 = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times (2000)^2$$

$$E_0 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{6}$$

$$E_0 = 100 \text{ J}$$

**(ii) Peak current:**

$$I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_0}{R} = \dfrac{2000}{50} = 40 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) RC time constant:**

$$\tau = RC = 50 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ ms}$$

**(iv) Voltage after 5 ms:**

$$V(5\text{ ms}) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau} = 2000 \cdot e^{-5/2.5} = 2000 \cdot e^{-2}$$

$$V(5\text{ ms}) = 2000 \times 0.1353 = 270.7 \text{ V}$$

**(v) Energy delivered in first 5 ms:**

$$E(5\text{ ms}) = E_0 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-2t/\tau}\right) = 100 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-4}\right)$$

$$E(5\text{ ms}) = 100 \times (1 - 0.0183) = 100 \times 0.9817 = 98.17 \text{ J}$$

> **Interpretation:** 98.17% of the stored energy is delivered in 5 ms — confirms that the standard discharge duration of 4–12 ms is sufficient to deliver virtually all stored energy.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Defibrillator and Differentiate Between Monophasic and Biphasic Waveforms. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **defibrillator** is an electromedical device that delivers a controlled electrical shock to the heart to terminate life-threatening arrhythmias such as ventricular fibrillation (VF) and pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT). The shock depolarizes the myocardium simultaneously, allowing the SA node to resume normal sinus rhythm.

| Feature | Monophasic | Biphasic |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity | Single direction (one polarity) | Two phases (positive then negative) |
| Current flow | One direction through heart | Reverses direction mid-pulse |
| Energy required for same efficacy | Higher (200–360 J) | Lower (120–200 J) |
| Myocardial injury | Greater | Significantly less |
| Impedance compensation | Poor | Active, adaptive |
| Modern standard | Obsolete | **Current clinical standard** |

**(3 marks)** ✓

---

### Question 2: What is Transthoracic Impedance and Why is It Important? **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Transthoracic Impedance (TTI)** is the total electrical resistance offered by the chest wall (skin, fat, muscle, ribs, lungs, and heart tissue) to the flow of defibrillating current. It typically ranges from **25 Ω to 150 Ω**, with a clinical standard of **50 Ω** used for testing.

**Importance:**
1. **Determines peak current** $I_{peak} = V_0 / R_{TTI}$ for a given stored voltage.
2. **Affects energy delivery** — higher impedance means less current, less effective defibrillation.
3. **Influences waveform selection** — biphasic waveforms automatically adjust duration to compensate.
4. **Reduced by:** conductive gel, firm paddle pressure (≈ 8 kg in adults), proper pad placement, and shock sequencing.

**(3 marks)** ✓

### **Question A (Choice 1): Dissection Study of a Defibrillator — Component Identification, Working, and Energy Calculations** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a defibrillator. Identify the major components you would observe during a dissection study.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction and Working:**

A defibrillator consists of six major functional blocks:

1. **Low-Voltage Power Supply (SMPS):** Converts 220 V AC mains to multiple DC rails (+5 V, ±15 V) for the control electronics, microcontroller, and display. A **12 V / 4 Ah SLA battery** provides portable/backup power.

2. **High-Voltage DC-DC Converter:** A high-frequency oscillator (20–50 kHz) drives a step-up transformer with turns ratio ≈ 1:100, producing HV AC which is then rectified to DC.

3. **Energy Storage Capacitor (ESC):** A **32 μF / 3600 V** metallized polypropylene film capacitor stores the energy. Voltage feedback is taken via a high-resistance divider for the front-panel meter.

4. **Charge/Discharge Switch:** An **IGBT** or **thyristor** controlled by the microcontroller connects the ESC to the waveform-shaping network on operator command.

5. **Waveform Shaping Network:** For monophasic defibrillators, an inductor (L ≈ 30 mH) and resistor network form the **Lown damped sinusoidal** waveform. For biphasic, an **H-bridge** of four IGBTs reverses polarity for the second phase.

6. **Patient Interface:** Hard paddles or self-adhesive pads connected via a high-voltage patient cable with an **optical isolation barrier** (≥ 5000 V dielectric withstand) to protect the ECG amplifier.

**Components observed during dissection (7 components × 1 mark each):**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | HV capacitor (cylindrical, 32 μF) | Energy storage |
| 2 | HV step-up transformer (ferrite core) | Voltage step-up |
| 3 | HV rectifier diode (high reverse-voltage) | AC to HV-DC |
| 4 | IGBT/SCR module (TO-247 case) | Solid-state switching |
| 5 | Inductor coil (toroidal) | Waveform shaping |
| 6 | Microcontroller PCB (SMD) | System control |
| 7 | LCD display + keypad | User interface |

**(7 marks)** ✓

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A defibrillator uses a 32 μF capacitor charged to 2500 V. It discharges through a 50 Ω patient. Calculate: (i) energy stored, (ii) peak discharge current, (iii) RC time constant, (iv) time for voltage to decay to 500 V, (v) energy delivered in that time.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Energy stored:**

$$E_0 = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times (2500)^2$$

$$E_0 = 0.5 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.25 \times 10^{6} = 100 \text{ J}$$

**(ii) Peak current:**

$$I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_0}{R} = \dfrac{2500}{50} = 50 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) RC time constant:**

$$\tau = RC = 50 \times 32 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.6 \text{ ms}$$

**(iv) Time for V to decay to 500 V:**

$$V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau} \;\Rightarrow\; 500 = 2500 \cdot e^{-t/1.6\text{ms}}$$

$$e^{-t/1.6\text{ms}} = 0.2$$

$$-t/1.6\text{ms} = \ln(0.2) = -1.609$$

$$t = 1.6 \times 1.609 = 2.575 \text{ ms}$$

**(v) Energy delivered up to that time:**

$$E(t) = E_0 \left(1 - e^{-2t/\tau}\right) = 100 \left(1 - e^{-2 \times 2.575 / 1.6}\right)$$

$$E(t) = 100 \left(1 - e^{-3.22}\right) = 100 \times (1 - 0.0399) = 96.01 \text{ J}$$

**Results Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Energy stored | **100 J** |
| Peak current | **50 A** |
| Time constant | **1.6 ms** |
| Decay time to 500 V | **2.58 ms** |
| Energy delivered | **96.01 J (96% of stored)** |

**(7 marks)** ✓

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): Synchronization, AED Algorithm, and Safety Features** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the synchronization circuit used in a cardioversion defibrillator. How does it differ from an Automated External Defibrillator (AED)?

**Model Answer:**

**Synchronization Circuit in Cardioversion:**

In synchronized cardioversion, the shock is delivered at a precisely chosen moment of the cardiac cycle to avoid the **vulnerable T-wave period** (which could trigger VF). The working principle:

1. **ECG Acquisition:** The patient's ECG is sensed through the same paddles/pads or separate monitoring electrodes.
2. **R-Wave Detection:** A high-pass filter, comparator, and peak-detector circuit identify the QRS complex. The R-wave is recognized by its steepest positive slope.
3. **Synchronization Delay:** A monostable multivibrator introduces a **30 ms delay** to ensure the shock is delivered at the absolute peak of R (not on its upstroke).
4. **Discharge Trigger:** The microcontroller then fires the IGBT/SCR switch — the storage capacitor dumps its energy through the patient.
5. **Indicator:** A "SYNC" LED on the front panel flashes each time a shock is delivered in sync.

**Difference Between Cardioversion and AED:**

| Parameter | Synchronized Cardioversion | AED |
|---|---|---|
| **Operator** | Trained clinician | Lay rescuer / public |
| **Rhythm detection** | Operator decides | Internal algorithm (DFT, VFT) |
| **Energy selection** | Operator-selected (e.g., 100 J, 200 J) | Pre-programmed escalating protocol |
| **Shock timing** | Synchronized to R-wave | Asynchronous (no sync for VF) |
| **Patient condition** | Stable VT, AF, SVT, atrial flutter | Cardiac arrest with VF/pVT |
| **Voice prompts** | None | Yes — guides rescuer step-by-step |
| **Impedance measurement** | Optional | Standard, automatic |

**(7 marks)** ✓

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Describe the safety features incorporated in a modern defibrillator. Why is patient isolation essential?

**Model Answer:**

**Safety Features in a Modern Defibrillator:**

1. **Patient Isolation Barrier:**
   - **Optical isolation** (opto-couplers) between the patient-connected ECG amplifier and the rest of the device, rated for ≥ 5000 V dielectric withstand.
   - Prevents any leakage current from the mains reaching the patient.
   - Essential because the patient may have wet skin, IV lines, or be in contact with grounded metal.

2. **Defibrillator-Proof ECG Amplifier:**
   - Protected by a network of **diodes, MOVs (metal-oxide varistors), and TVS diodes** that clamp the high-voltage discharge pulse and prevent the ECG front-end from being damaged.
   - Recovers to normal operation within seconds after the shock.

3. **Bleeder Resistor:**
   - A **5–10 MΩ high-voltage resistor** permanently connected across the storage capacitor.
   - Automatically discharges the capacitor if the device is switched off or the lid is opened (when interlocked).

4. **Charge Time Limit:**
   - The HV converter is automatically disabled if the capacitor does not reach the set voltage within 30 seconds (per IEC 60601-2-4).

5. **Synchronization Lockout:**
   - In sync mode, the shock is inhibited unless a valid R-wave is detected within the last 5 seconds — preventing accidental discharge on a T-wave.

6. **Internal Discharge on Shutdown:**
   - When the device is switched off, the microcontroller commands an automatic internal discharge to a safe level (< 5 J).

7. **Battery Low Indication:**
   - Prevents unreliable operation. The device refuses to deliver a shock if the battery voltage drops below a critical threshold.

8. **Self-Test Routine:**
   - Daily automatic self-test of the HV circuit, ECG amplifier, and battery — logs results internally.

**Why Patient Isolation is Essential:**

Without proper isolation, the defibrillation current could:
- Flow through the ECG amplifier, destroying it.
- Leak into the operator, causing serious injury.
- Combine with other medical devices (e.g., pacemaker leads) and cause burns.
- Create a ground-loop current path, increasing the risk of microshock (current > 10 μA directly to the myocardium is lethal).

**(7 marks)** ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the bleeder resistor** — A common 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Not specifying the 50 Ω standard test load** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Confusing cardioversion (synchronized) with defibrillation (asynchronous).** Cardioversion → AF, SVT; Defibrillation → VF, pVT.
> 4. **Writing "shock restarts the heart"** — WRONG! It *depolarizes* the myocardium to allow the SA node to resume.
> 5. **Missing safety steps in dissection** — Always mention bleeder discharge first.
> 6. **In energy calculations, omitting the factor 1/2** — Double-check $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Energy formula:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ — the *defining equation* of defibrillator energy budget.
- ✅ **Standard test load:** **50 Ω** (IEC 60601-2-4).
- ✅ **Common capacitor values:** 32 μF and 50 μF.
- ✅ **Voltage range:** 0–3600 V.
- ✅ **Energy levels:** Adult 2–360 J; Pediatric 2–100 J.
- ✅ **Two waveform types:** **Monophasic** (Lown damped sinusoid, obsolete) and **Biphasic** (truncated exponential, modern standard).
- ✅ **Biphasic advantage:** Lower energy, less myocardial damage, better impedance compensation.
- ✅ **RC time constant:** $\tau = R_{TTI} \cdot C$.
- ✅ **V(t) decay:** $V(t) = V_0 e^{-t/\tau}$.
- ✅ **Peak current:** $I_{peak} = V_0 / R_{TTI}$.
- ✅ **Synchronized shock** = delivered at R-peak; **Asynchronous** = immediate.
- ✅ **AED** = automated rhythm analysis, voice prompts, pre-set energy escalation.
- ✅ **Bleeder resistor** = 5–10 MΩ across HV capacitor for safety discharge.
- ✅ **Patient isolation** = opto-couplers rated for ≥ 5000 V.
- ✅ **Six dissection blocks:** SMPS, HV converter, storage capacitor, charge/discharge switch, waveform shaping network, patient interface.
- ✅ **Components to identify during dissection:** HV capacitor, HV transformer, HV diode, IGBT/SCR, inductor, microcontroller PCB, LCD, paddles/pads.
- ✅ **Charge time to 360 J** = < 7 seconds (IEC specification).
- ✅ **Discharge duration:** 4–12 ms (biphasic), 12–20 ms (monophasic).
- ✅ **Defibrillator does NOT restart the heart** — it depolarizes the myocardium to reset the SA node.
- ✅ **Pre-dissection safety:** discharge capacitor via 10 kΩ bleeder, wait 5 minutes, verify zero voltage with HV probe.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
