# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "Commissioning" of a biomedical equipment system. List the THREE mandatory tests performed during commissioning as per IEC 62353.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Commissioning is the formal, documented verification process performed immediately after installation to demonstrate that a new or refurbished biomedical equipment meets the manufacturer's specifications, the purcher's purchase-order requirements, and the relevant safety standards before being released for clinical use.

**Three Mandatory Tests (2 marks — ½ + ½ + ½ + ½):**

1. **Protective Earth Resistance Test** — verifies the integrity of the protective earth conductor; limit ≤ 0.3 Ω for Class I equipment with applied part.
2. **Insulation Resistance Test** — measures leakage between live parts and earth; limit ≥ 2 MΩ for Class I equipment.
3. **Earth Leakage Current Test** — measures current flowing in the protective earth conductor under normal and single-fault conditions; limit ≤ 500 μA (NC) / ≤ 1 mA (SFC) per IEC 62353.
4. **Patient Leakage Current Test** — measures current flowing through the patient-applied part; limit ≤ 100 μA (Type BF).

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Pattern Decoded:** KTU award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the list (½ mark each). Memorise the numeric thresholds — they are asked as "fill in the blanks" in part A.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Q: Differentiate between "Preventive Maintenance" and "Predictive Maintenance" of biomedical equipment. Give ONE example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Predictive Maintenance (PdM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Calendar or usage-hour interval (time-based) | Condition-monitoring data (condition-based) |
| **Philosophy** | "Replace before failure" | "Intervene just before failure" |
| **Records** | Logbook with dated entries | Continuous trend graph in CMMS |
| **Cost** | Predictable but may be wasteful | Higher setup cost, lower long-term cost |

**Examples (2 marks):**
- **PM:** Quarterly replacement of ventilator bacterial filters and O₂ sensor calibration every 6 months.
- **PdM:** Battery Impedance Spectroscopy on defibrillators — battery replaced when internal resistance crosses a threshold, not at fixed date.

> [!TIP]
> Always conclude the Part-A answer with a *single comparative sentence* — evaluators award the "**comparison**" mark only when both are referenced in the same line.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q: During a field visit to a 350-bed NABH-accredited multi-specialty hospital, you are asked to audit the ICU and Radiology departments.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Prepare a **comprehensive workflow protocol** for the ICU, covering: patient admission, monitoring, infection control, medication administration, and discharge/transfer. Annotate the regulatory standards referenced at each stage.

**(b) [7 marks]** Draw the **facility layout** of a typical CT-scan room suite (CT room, control room, equipment room, patient preparation area), showing dimensions, lead-shielded walls, door interlocks, HVAC pressure relationships, and MGPS outlets. List the **AERB regulatory requirements** that the layout must satisfy.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a)

**ICU Workflow Protocol (7 marks — 1 mark per workflow stage + 1 mark for regulatory mapping):**

**Step 1 — Patient Admission from OT / ED / Ward (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Handover SBAR} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Identity Verification 2 identifiers} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ICU Admission Checklist NABH AAC Std}
$$
Documentation: Admission note, initial nursing assessment within 15 minutes, ICU-specific consent.

**Step 2 — Continuous Monitoring (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Multi-Parameter Monitor ECG SpO2 NIBP Temp RR} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Central Nursing Station Display} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CMMS Alarm Logging}
$$
Regulatory: **IEC 60601-2-49** for multifunction patient monitoring equipment; alarm-limit settings as per manufacturer.

**Step 3 — Infection Control (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Hand Hygiene 5 Moments WHO} \;\rightarrow\; \text{PPE Donning Doffing SOP} \;\rightarrow\; \text{HVAC Positive 10 Pa HEPA H13}
$$
Regulatory: **NABH COP Std on Infection Control**, **IS 14455** for ICU design, BMW Rules 2016 for waste segregation.

**Step 4 — Medication Administration (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Prescription CPOE} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Pharmacy Verification} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Nurse 5 Rights at Bedside} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Smart Infusion Pump Drug Library}
$$
Regulatory: **NABH COP Std on Medication Safety**, infusion pump must comply with **IEC 60601-2-24**.

**Step 5 — Ventilator / Life-Support Management (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Ventilator Setup per Protocol} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Daily Weaning Assessment} \;\rightarrow\; \text{PM Schedule per OEM}
$$
Regulatory: **IEC 60601-2-12** for ventilators, daily self-test mandatory.

**Step 6 — Discharge / Transfer (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Discharge Criteria Stable} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ISBAR Handover to Ward} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Equipment Cleaning Terminal Disinfection} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Documentation Discharge Summary}
$$
Regulatory: **NABH COP Std on Continuum of Care**.

**Step 7 — Regulatory Cross-Reference Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Workflow Stage | Applicable Regulation |
|---|---|
| Admission | NABH AAC Std 1 |
| Monitoring | IEC 60601-2-49 |
| Infection Control | NABH COP, IS 14455, BMW Rules 2016 |
| Medication | NABH COP, IEC 60601-2-24 |
| Ventilator | IEC 60601-2-12 |
| Discharge | NABH COP |

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b)

**CT-Suite Facility Layout & AERB Compliance (7 marks):**

**Block Schematic (3 marks) — to be drawn by student:**

```
+----------------+    +----------------+    +----------------+
|   PATIENT      |    |   CT SCAN      |    |   EQUIPMENT    |
|   PREP AREA    |--->|   ROOM         |    |   ROOM         |
|   (8 m²)       |    |   (35 m²)      |    |   (8 m²)       |
+----------------+    +----------------+    +----------------+
                              |                    |
                              v                    v
                       +----------------+    +----------------+
                       |   CONTROL      |    |   UPS / MGPS   |
                       |   ROOM         |    |   MANIFOLD     |
                       |   (10 m²)      |    |   (6 m²)       |
                       +----------------+    +----------------+
```

**Dimensions & Tolerances (1 mark):**
- CT room: minimum **35 m²** floor area, **3.0 m** ceiling height.
- Walls: **150 mm RCC + 2 mm lead equivalent** (primary barrier); viewing window **≥ 2 mm Pb** glass.
- Door: **≥ 1.5 m wide**, **2 mm lead equivalent**, with **electromagnetic interlock** to prevent exposure when door is open.

**AERB Regulatory Requirements (3 marks — 1 mark each cluster):**

1. **Site Approval:** AERB e-LORA registration of the X-ray installation before commissioning; Layout plan approved by AERB.
2. **Personnel:** Qualified radiologist (AERB-approved) and AERB-certified radiographer mandatory on duty.
3. **Quality Assurance:** Annual QA audit by AERB-authorised agency covering kVp accuracy, mA linearity, slice thickness, CTDI<sub>vol</sub>, image uniformity. Calibration traceability to national standards (NABL).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to specify the **lead equivalent thickness** for the primary barrier. Awarding the layout mark depends on this numeric citation. Also, **failure to mention the door interlock** is a 1-mark cut.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: As part of the PCBML607 Module Experiment 2, you visit a hospital's Biomedical Engineering Department (BMED) and CSSD.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Outline the **standard operating procedure (SOP)** for the **preventive maintenance of a multi-parameter patient monitor** as practiced by the BMED. Cite the **IEC standards** that govern each maintenance step, and prepare a **sample PM checklist**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **CSSD workflow** from point of use to sterile storage, identifying the **critical control points (CCPs)**. State the **physical and chemical indicators** used in steam sterilization validation and their acceptable pass/fail criteria.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (a)

**PM SOP for Multi-Parameter Patient Monitor (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Pre-PM Preparation (1 mark):**
- Notify clinical staff; isolate from patient; arrange backup monitor.
- Reference: **OEM Service Manual** + **IEC 62353** recurrent test protocol.

**Step 2 — Visual Inspection (1 mark):**
- Housing integrity, cable insulation (no cracks), display pixels, label legibility, battery contacts, fuse rating.

**Step 3 — Electrical Safety Tests per IEC 62353 (2 marks):**
- Protective Earth Resistance: ≤ 0.3 Ω (Class I).
- Insulation Resistance: ≥ 2 MΩ between mains and earth.
- Earth Leakage Current (NC + SFC): ≤ 500 μA / ≤ 1 mA.
- Patient Leakage Current (BF applied part): ≤ 100 μA / ≤ 500 μA.
- Dielectric Strength: 1500 V AC for 1 minute (Class I).

**Step 4 — Performance Verification (2 marks):**
- ECG simulator: amplitude accuracy ± 5 %, rate accuracy ± 1 bpm, lead-off detection.
- SpO<sub>2</sub>: functional tester with Masimo/Nellcor setting; verify plethysmograph and pulse-rate tracking.
- NIBP: against calibrated manometer (static pressure accuracy ± 3 mmHg); leakage rate < 6 mmHg/min.
- Temperature: ± 0.1 °C against reference at 37 °C and 41 °C.

**Step 5 — Calibration & Documentation (1 mark):**
- Calibration certificate (NABL traceable if in-house lab; OEM if outsourced).
- Update PM sticker on device; logbook entry with date, technician signature, next PM due date.
- IEC 60601-2-49 for multi-function monitoring equipment; IEC 60601-1 for general safety.

**Sample PM Checklist (for student to draw as table):**

| Sl | Test | Standard | Limit | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earth Resistance | IEC 62353 | ≤ 0.3 Ω | ___ |
| 2 | Insulation | IEC 62353 | ≥ 2 MΩ | ___ |
| 3 | Earth Leakage NC | IEC 62353 | ≤ 500 μA | ___ |
| 4 | Patient Leakage BF | IEC 62353 | ≤ 100 μA | ___ |
| 5 | NIBP Static | IEC 80601-2-30 | ± 3 mmHg | ___ |
| 6 | SpO<sub>2</sub> Functional | ISO 80601-2-61 | ± 3 % | ___ |
| 7 | Battery Backup | IEC 62133 | ≥ 90 min | ___ |

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (b)

**CSSD Workflow & Sterilization Validation (7 marks):**

**Workflow Stages with CCPs (4 marks — ½ mark each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Point of Use Pre-Clean} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{[CCP-1: Prevent drying of biological soil, max 15 min]} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Transportation Closed Trolley} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{[CCP-2: Avoid aerosol generation, no spill]} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Receiving and Sorting} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{[CCP-3: PPE donning, sharps count]} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Manual Cleaning} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{[CCP-4: Enzymatic detergent, brush all lumens]} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Ultrasonic Cleaning 5 min} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{[CCP-5: 40 kHz, degassing done]} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Rinsing and Drying} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{[CCP-6: RO/DI water for final rinse]} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Inspection and Packaging} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{[CCP-7: Magnification 5×, weight check for trays]} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Steam Sterilization 121 °C / 134 °C} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{[CCP-8: Process Challenge Device]} \\
\downarrow \\
\text{Sterile Storage Clean Zone Positive Pressure} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{[CCP-9: Temperature 18–22 °C, RH 35–60 \%]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steam Sterilization Indicators (2 marks):**

| Indicator Type | Function | Pass Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical (Class I)** | Gauges on sterilizer: temperature, pressure, time chart | 121 °C / 15 psi / 15 min or 134 °C / 30 psi / 3 min |
| **Chemical (Class IV)** | Multi-parameter integrator strips on each pack | Colour change from yellow to dark — confirms all 3 critical variables met |
| **Chemical (Class V)** | Emulating indicator (integrator) | Same as Class IV but for specific cycle |
| **Biological (Class VI) — Geobacillus stearothermophilus spore vial** | Gold standard viability test | **No growth** in incubated vial after 48 h at 56 °C = **PASS**; any growth = **FAIL** (sterilizer taken out of service) |

**Frequency of Indicator Use (1 mark):**
- **Bowie-Dick Test:** Daily, before first cycle of the day.
- **Biological Indicator:** Weekly (per AERB/NABH recommendation).
- **Chemical Class IV:** Every pack.
- **Class I Physical:** Every cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly **omit the Bowie-Dick Test** or fail to state that a *failed Bowie-Dick* means the sterilizer is immediately taken out of service. This is the single most critical CSSD safety concept — losing 1 full mark here is the difference between A and B grade.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Bullet Bank — Revise Before Every KTU Exam on This Module.**

### A. Regulatory Bodies (Memorise with 1-line anchor)

- **NABH** → Hospital-wide accreditation, voluntary but de-facto mandatory.
- **AERB** → Radiation equipment (X-ray, CT, MRI-gradient? no, MRI is non-ionising but still regulated for safety), AERB Safety Code mandatory.
- **CDSCO** → Medical device registration under MDR 2017.
- **IEC 60601-1** → General electrical safety of electromedical equipment.
- **IEC 62353** → Recurrent / post-repair testing in service.
- **ISO 13485** → Manufacturer QMS.
- **ISO 14971** → Risk management process.
- **BMWM Rules 2016** → Yellow / Red / White / Blue containers.
- **PNDT Act** → Ultrasound registration, Form F.
- **NABL 113 / ISO 17025** → Calibration lab accreditation.

### B. Equipment Life-Cycle

$$
\text{Need} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Procure} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Install} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Commission} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Use} \;\rightarrow\; \text{PM} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Calibrate} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Repair} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Decommission}
$$

### C. Three-Pillar Report Formula

$$
\text{Workflow (5-step)} \;+\; \text{Regulation (named citation)} \;+\; \text{Layout (with dimensions)} \;=\; \text{Full Marks}
$$

### D. Key Numerical Constants to Memorise

| Constant | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Earth resistance (Class I) | ≤ 0.3 Ω | IEC 62353 |
| Patient leakage (BF, NC) | ≤ 100 μA | IEC 62353 |
| OT air changes per hour | 20–25 ACH | IS 14455 / NABH |
| CSSD clean zone pressure | +10 Pa | IS 14455 |
| OT temperature | 21 ± 3 °C | NABH |
| OT RH | 20–60 % | NABH |
| Bowie-Dick test | 134 °C / 3.5 min daily | EN 13060 / EN 285 |
| Steam sterilization | 121 °C / 15 psi / 15 min or 134 °C / 30 psi / 3 min | EN 13060 |
| Sterile storage RH | 35–60 % | NABH |

### E. Most-Asked Viva Questions (Top 5)

1. *"Which IEC standard governs recurrent testing of electromedical equipment?"* → **IEC 62353**.
2. *"What is the patient leakage current limit for Type BF equipment in NC condition?"* → **100 μA**.
3. *"Which AERB document governs CT installation?"* → **AERB Safety Code on Medical Applications of Radiation**.
4. *"What is the pressure relationship in an OT?"* → **Positive (+12.5 Pa)**.
5. *"Which colour container for chemical waste?"* → **Yellow** (per BMW Rules 2016, Schedule I).

### F. The 5 Errors That Cost the Most Marks

1. Drawing layout **without dimensions** (-2 marks).
2. Citing a regulation **without mapping it to equipment** (-1 mark).
3. Confusing **IEC 60601** (design) with **IEC 62353** (in-service) (-1 mark).
4. Missing **Bowie-Dick Test** in CSSD answer (-1 mark).
5. Writing *"infection control is done"* without naming WHO 5 Moments / NABH Std (-1 mark).

### G. Department Visits — What to Always Photograph

1. Equipment **PM sticker** (shows last/next PM date).
2. **Calibration certificate** (NABL logo visible).
3. **NABH display board** at reception.
4. **AERB Type Approval label** on X-ray unit.
5. **Colour-coded BMW bins** (yellow/red/white/blue).
6. **MGPS outlet** with zone valve box.
7. **Electrical panel** in plant room.
8. **OT HEPA filter** with pressure gauge.
9. **CSSD Bowie-Dick test record** from morning log.
10. **Nurse-call system master station**.

### H. One-Line Mantras for Last-Minute Recall

> *"No equipment is installed without a **commissioning certificate**."*
> *"No regulatory citation is complete without a **clause number**."*
> *"No layout is complete without a **door-swing arrow and one dimension**."*
> *"No CSSD cycle is complete without a **Bowie-Dick**."*
> *"No PM is complete without a **logbook entry + sticker**."*

**— End of KTU 2024 Module Experiment 2 Notes —**

topics.title: Dissect and familiarize with an ultrasound machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBML607 - PCBML607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Dissect and familiarize with an ultrasound machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **diagnostic ultrasound machine (Ultrasonography / USG scanner)** is a non-invasive, radiation-free medical imaging device that uses **high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically 2 MHz to 15 MHz)** to visualize internal body structures in real time. The system operates on the **pulse-echo principle** combined with the **piezoelectric effect**, where a transducer emits ultrasonic pulses into tissue and receives the reflected echoes to construct a 2-D, 3-D, or Doppler-based image of anatomical regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *An ultrasound machine is an electroacoustic biomedical imaging instrument that converts electrical energy into high-frequency sound waves via a piezoelectric transducer, transmits these waves into the body, captures the returning echoes, and processes them using time-of-flight calculations to generate diagnostic images of soft tissues, organs, blood flow, and fetuses.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of an ultrasound machine as **"SONAR for the human body,"** exactly like how a bat navigates in pitch-dark caves:

1. A **bat (transducer)** emits a sharp **squeak (ultrasound pulse)**.
2. The sound **bounces off walls (tissue interfaces)** and returns as an **echo**.
3. The bat's brain **calculates the delay** between squeak and echo to know the **distance and shape** of the wall.
4. By making thousands of such squeaks per second in different directions, the bat builds a **mental 3-D map of the cave (the sonographic image)**.

The ultrasound machine does the same: it shouts (pulses), listens (echoes), times them (microseconds), and paints the picture on a monitor.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
> [!NOTE]
> - **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** $c \approx 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ (standard medical value used by all scanners)
> - **Diagnostic frequency range:** **2 MHz – 15 MHz**
> - **Transducer element material:** Lead Zirconate Titanate (**PZT**)
> - **Acoustic impedance of soft tissue:** $Z \approx 1.6 \times 10^6 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ (rayl)
> - **Image frame rate (real time):** 15 – 60 frames/second
> - **Acoustic output safety limit (FDA):** Spatial Peak Temporal Average Intensity $\leq 720 \, \text{mW/cm}^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Breakdown
The ultrasound machine functions as a **sequential transmit-receive (T/R) system** that repeats thousands of times per second. Each cycle is called a **scan line** or **pulse repetition cycle**.

**Step 1 – Pulse Generation (Transmitter Stage):**
The pulser applies a high-voltage, short-duration electrical spike (~100–200 V, ~0.1–1 μs) across the piezoelectric crystal, causing it to mechanically deform and emit an ultrasound burst at the desired frequency.

**Step 2 – Beam Formation & Steering:**
The beam is shaped (focused, steered, or swept) using either a **mechanical sector scanner** (rotating/oscillating transducer) or an **electronic phased-array / linear-array** system that fires elements with microsecond delays.

**Step 3 – Propagation Through Tissue:**
The pulse travels at **$c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$** through tissue, undergoing partial reflection at every acoustic-impedance mismatch (e.g., muscle–fat, blood–vessel-wall, soft-tissue–bone).

**Step 4 – Echo Reception (Receiver Stage):**
Returning echoes re-deform the same crystal (reversed piezoelectric effect), producing tiny voltages (microvolts) that are amplified by a **low-noise, time-gain-compensated (TGC) receiver**.

**Step 5 – Signal Processing:**
The amplified RF (radio-frequency) signal is **envelope-detected, log-compressed, scan-converted** from polar (sector) to Cartesian (rectangular) coordinates, and displayed.

**Step 6 – Display:**
A grayscale or color-flow image is rendered on a monitor. Brighter pixels = stronger echoes (e.g., bone, organ boundaries). Darker pixels = no echo (e.g., fluid in bladder, cyst).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Every Block
| Subsystem | Why It Exists |
|-----------|---------------|
| Pulser | Need a sharp, high-energy burst to penetrate tissue |
| Transducer | Converts electrical $\leftrightarrow$ acoustic energy bidirectionally |
| TGC amplifier | Compensates for attenuation so deep echoes are as bright as shallow ones |
| Scan converter | Aligns polar acquisition data to a rectangular TV display |
| Doppler processor | Extracts velocity info for blood-flow imaging |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Depth of reflecting interface (round-trip time $t$) | m |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot f \cdot n}$ | Axial (range) resolution (n = cycles per pulse) | m |
| 3 | $L = 1.22 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda \cdot D}{d}$ | Lateral resolution (aperture $D$, element width $d$) | m |
| 4 | $\text{PRF}_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot D_{\max}}$ | Maximum pulse repetition frequency (to avoid range ambiguity) | Hz |
| 5 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift (blood-flow velocity $v$, beam angle $\theta$) | Hz |
| 6 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic impedance (characteristic impedance of medium) | rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) |
| 7 | $\alpha = \alpha_0 \cdot f^n$ | Attenuation coefficient ($\alpha_0 \approx 0.5$ dB/cm/MHz, $n \approx 1$) | dB/cm |
| 8 | $I_r = I_i \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ | Reflection coefficient (intensity) at an interface | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
Ultrasound is the **safest cross-sectional imaging modality** (no ionizing radiation, bedside-portable, real-time, low-cost) and is the modality of choice in **obstetrics, cardiology (echocardiography), emergency medicine (FAST scans), vascular imaging, and musculoskeletal diagnosis**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Depth of a Reflecting Interface
The fundamental ranging equation that allows the scanner to localize every echo in depth.

$$
\text{Let } t = \text{time elapsed between transmitted pulse and received echo (s)}
$$

$$
\text{Let } c = \text{speed of sound in soft tissue} \approx 1540 \, \text{m/s}
$$

The pulse travels a **total round-trip distance** of:

$$
D_{\text{round-trip}} = c \cdot t
$$

Since the tissue interface is only **halfway** from the transducer (the pulse had to go down AND come back up), the **actual depth** of the reflector is:

$$
\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A patient undergoes an abdominal scan. The scanner records an echo at time $t = 130 \, \mu\text{s}$. Find the depth of the reflecting interface.

$$
d = \frac{1540 \, \text{m/s} \times 130 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.2002}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{d = 0.1001 \, \text{m} \approx 10 \, \text{cm}}
$$

This is the depth of, for example, the kidney or the upper edge of a deep organ.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Axial (Range) Resolution
Axial resolution is the **minimum distance along the beam axis** at which two reflectors can be distinguished as separate.

$$
\text{A pulse of } n \text{ cycles at frequency } f \text{ has a spatial length:} \quad L_{\text{pulse}} = n \cdot \lambda
$$

$$
\text{But } \lambda = \frac{c}{f}, \text{ therefore:} \quad L_{\text{pulse}} = \frac{n \cdot c}{f}
$$

For two echoes to be **just separable**, the second echo must arrive **after the first pulse has completely died down**, which requires the second reflector to be at least $L_{\text{pulse}}/2$ deeper (the factor of 2 accounts for the round trip):

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{axial}} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot f \cdot n}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
For a 5 MHz transducer with $n = 2$ cycles per pulse:

$$
R_{\text{axial}} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 2} = \frac{1540}{20 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
R_{\text{axial}} = 77 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 0.077 \, \text{mm} \approx 77 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

This is why **higher frequency = better resolution = poorer penetration** (a fundamental trade-off in ultrasound).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Maximum Pulse Repetition Frequency
To avoid **range ambiguity** (a new pulse being transmitted before the deepest echo from the previous pulse has returned), the time between pulses must be $\geq$ the round-trip time to the deepest target of interest, $D_{\max}$:

$$
T_{\min} \geq \frac{2 \cdot D_{\max}}{c}
$$

Therefore the maximum pulse repetition frequency is:

$$
\boxed{\text{PRF}_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot D_{\max}}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
For abdominal imaging with $D_{\max} = 20 \, \text{cm} = 0.2 \, \text{m}$:

$$
\text{PRF}_{\max} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.2} = \frac{1540}{0.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PRF}_{\max} = 3850 \, \text{Hz}}
$$

Exceeding this PRF would cause the system to misinterpret the previous pulse's deep echo as a shallow echo from the new pulse — a critical **design constraint** enforced in every clinical scanner.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 – Doppler Frequency Shift (Blood-Flow Velocity)
For a wave reflecting off a moving red blood cell, the frequency received is shifted from the transmitted frequency $f_0$. The general Doppler formula is:

$$
f_d = f_r - f_0
$$

For a stationary source, moving reflector (blood cell), then moving source (the cell re-radiates), applying the Doppler effect twice and accounting for the beam-to-flow angle $\theta$:

$$
\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
A 5 MHz transducer is used to measure blood flow at $\theta = 60^\circ$. The measured Doppler shift is $f_d = 1500 \, \text{Hz}$. Find the blood-flow velocity.

$$
v = \frac{f_d \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

$$
v = \frac{1500 \times 1540}{2 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times \cos 60^\circ}
$$

$$
v = \frac{2{,}310{,}000}{10^7 \times 0.5} = \frac{2{,}310{,}000}{5 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{v = 0.462 \, \text{m/s} = 46.2 \, \text{cm/s}}
$$

This is a clinically normal range for arterial blood flow.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Diagnostic Ultrasound Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL[Master Controller and Keyboard]
        MCU[CPU and Scan Sequencer]
        KBD[Operator Keyboard and Trackball]
    end

    subgraph TX[Transmitter Section]
        PULSER[High Voltage Pulser 100 to 200 V]
        TRIG[Transmit Beamformer with Delay Lines]
    end

    subgraph TEE[Transducer Probe]
        ARRAY[2D Piezoelectric Array PZT]
    end

    subgraph RX[Receiver Section]
        TGC[Time Gain Compensation Amplifier]
        LNA[Low Noise Preamplifier]
        BF[Receive Beamformer with Dynamic Focusing]
        PROC[Envelope Detector and Log Compression]
    end

    subgraph DSP[Signal Processing and Display]
        SCAN[Scan Converter Polar to Cartesian]
        DOPPLER[Doppler and Color Flow Processor]
        MON[BW or Color LCD Monitor]
        PRINTER[Thermal or Laser Printer]
    end

    KBD --> MCU
    MCU --> PULSER
    MCU --> TRIG
    PULSER --> ARRAY
    ARRAY -. Echo Return Path .-> LNA
    LNA --> TGC
    TGC --> BF
    BF --> PROC
    PROC --> SCAN
    PROC --> DOPPLER
    SCAN --> MON
    DOPPLER --> MON
    MON --> PRINTER
    MCU -. Synchronization .-> SCAN
```

### 4.2 Signal-Flow Summary (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Pulse 0.1 to 1 microsecond] --> B[PZT Crystal Vibration]
    B --> C[Ultrasound Wave in Tissue 2 to 15 MHz]
    C --> D[Partial Reflection at Interfaces]
    D --> E[Returning Echo Strikes PZT]
    E --> F[Microvolt Electrical Signal]
    F --> G[TGC Amplification]
    G --> H[Beamforming and Focusing]
    H --> I[Envelope Detection]
    I --> J[Scan Conversion]
    J --> K[Grayscale or Color Image on Monitor]
```

### 4.3 Transducer Probe Anatomy (Dissection-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROBE[Ultrasound Probe Cross Section]
        CASE[Outer Plastic Case and Acoustic Lens]
        DAMPER[Backing Material High Attenuation Damping Layer]
        CRYSTAL[Active PZT Piezoelectric Element]
        MATCH[Matching Layer Quarter Wavelength Impedance Matcher]
        EDGE[Edge Liner for Suppression of Lateral Modes]
        CONN[Cable Connector and Shielded Coaxial Bundle to Scanner]
    end
    CASE --- DAMPER
    DAMPER --- CRYSTAL
    CRYSTAL --- MATCH
    CRYSTAL --- EDGE
    EDGE --- CONN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Anatomical correlation of block diagram during dissection:**
> When the housing is opened, the student will physically observe the **PZT element** (the heart of the system), the **damping backing** (which shortens the pulse and improves axial resolution), the **matching layer** (which minimizes the acoustic-impedance mismatch between PZT and tissue, maximizing energy transfer), and the **multi-element coaxial cable** that routes the 64–256 micro-coaxial channels from the array to the beamformer inside the main console.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define acoustic impedance and state its SI unit. Why is the matching layer essential in an ultrasound transducer?*

**Model Answer:**
Acoustic impedance $Z$ is the product of the density $\rho$ of a medium and the speed of sound $c$ in that medium:

$$
Z = \rho \cdot c
$$

SI unit: **kilogram per square meter per second (rayl)**. The matching layer (usually $\lambda/4$ thickness) has an intermediate impedance between the PZT crystal ($Z \approx 30 \times 10^6$ rayl) and soft tissue ($Z \approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ rayl) to maximize acoustic energy transmission into the body and minimize reflection at the probe–skin interface. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List the major subsystems visible when the front panel of a clinical ultrasound machine is opened. Mention the function of any two.*

**Model Answer:**
Major subsystems: (i) Pulser, (ii) Receiver with TGC, (iii) Beamformer, (iv) Scan converter, (v) Doppler processor, (vi) Master CPU/control board, (vii) Power supply, (viii) Monitor and user-interface PCB. The **pulser** generates the high-voltage spike that excites the PZT crystal. The **TGC amplifier** compensates for depth-dependent attenuation so that echoes from deep tissues appear with the same brightness as shallow echoes. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a diagnostic ultrasound machine. Label all blocks and describe the function of the transmitter, receiver, and display sections. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
*(Block diagram as in SECTION 4.1 above. The student should draw and label the following flow:)*

**Transmitter Section:** Generates a short, high-voltage electrical pulse (100–200 V, ~0.1–1 μs) and applies it to the PZT elements. The crystal vibrates at the desired resonant frequency (2–15 MHz) and emits an acoustic pulse. In a phased array, programmable delay lines steer and focus the beam electronically.

**Receiver Section:** Returning echoes deform the crystal and produce microvolt-level voltages. These are first amplified by a low-noise preamplifier, then passed through a **Time-Gain Compensator (TGC)** whose gain increases with time (i.e., with depth) to compensate for the $\sim 0.5$ dB/cm/MHz attenuation. After beamforming, the signal is envelope-detected and log-compressed (so 60 dB dynamic range fits on the screen).

**Display Section:** A scan converter transforms the polar (sector) data into Cartesian raster. The image is displayed on a monitor with grayscale (B-mode) or color overlay (Color Doppler). The frame rate is 15–60 Hz for real-time imaging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the depth-of-reflector equation $d = ct/2$. An ultrasound scanner detects an echo in $130 \, \mu\text{s}$. Calculate the depth of the reflector, assuming $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The pulse travels from the transducer to the reflector and back — a total round-trip distance of $c \cdot t$. The reflector itself is halfway, so the one-way depth is:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

Substituting $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ and $t = 130 \, \mu\text{s} = 130 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s}$:

$$
d = \frac{1540 \times 130 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.2002}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{d = 0.1001 \, \text{m} \approx 10 \, \text{cm}}
$$

The reflector is at a depth of **10 cm** (e.g., the upper pole of a kidney in an abdominal scan). **(7 marks)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of the piezoelectric effect. Describe the construction of an ultrasound probe with a labeled diagram and state the role of the matching layer and backing material. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Piezoelectric Effect:** When a mechanical stress is applied to certain crystals (PZT, quartz, PVDF), an electrical charge appears on their faces (direct effect), and conversely, when a voltage is applied, the crystal mechanically deforms (converse effect). The ultrasound probe uses this **reversible** phenomenon: voltage → sound (transmit) and sound → voltage (receive).

**Construction (from face to back):**
1. **Acoustic lens / matching layer** (λ/4 thick, $Z \approx 4$–$7 \times 10^6$ rayl): reduces the impedance mismatch between PZT and tissue, transmitting maximum acoustic energy into the body.
2. **Piezoelectric element** (PZT disc or array, 0.3–0.5 mm thick): the active element that generates and detects ultrasound.
3. **Backing / damping material** (tungsten–epoxy or similar): absorbs backward-traveling waves, shortens the pulse (fewer cycles), and therefore improves axial resolution.
4. **Outer casing** with the **multi-element coaxial cable** to the scanner. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With relevant equations, explain the principle of the Doppler ultrasound technique for measuring blood-flow velocity. A 5 MHz beam is incident on blood at $60^\circ$ and produces a Doppler shift of 1.5 kHz. Calculate the velocity of blood ($c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When ultrasound strikes a moving red blood cell, the frequency of the reflected wave is shifted by an amount proportional to the velocity of the cell. The complete Doppler equation (source stationary, reflector moving) is:

$$
f_d = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}
$$

where $f_0$ is the transmitted frequency, $v$ is the blood-flow velocity, $\theta$ is the angle between the beam and the flow direction, and $c$ is the speed of sound in tissue.

Rearranging for velocity:

$$
v = \frac{f_d \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

Substituting $f_d = 1500 \, \text{Hz}$, $f_0 = 5 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz}$, $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$, $\cos 60^\circ = 0.5$:

$$
v = \frac{1500 \times 1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5} = \frac{2{,}310{,}000}{5 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\boxed{v = 0.462 \, \text{m/s} = 46.2 \, \text{cm/s}}
$$

This value falls within the normal physiological range for arterial flow. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the depth equation $d = ct/2$ is the single most common mistake; always explain the round-trip logic.
> 2. **Mixing up axial and lateral resolution** – students often write $R_{\text{axial}} = c/f$ without the factor 2 and the $n$ cycles per pulse.
> 3. **Confusing acoustic impedance with electrical impedance** – acoustic impedance has units of rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹), NOT ohms.
> 4. **Drawing block diagrams without arrowheads** indicating signal flow – this costs at least 1 mark in 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Using the wrong speed of sound** – always use $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ in tissue; $c = 330 \, \text{m/s}$ is for air and is incorrect for medical ultrasound.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Speed of sound in soft tissue** $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ is a non-negotiable constant in every formula.
- [ ] **Depth equation** $d = ct/2$ — the **factor 2** is for the round-trip.
- [ ] **Axial resolution** $R = c/(2fn)$ — better at higher $f$ and fewer cycles per pulse.
- [ ] **Lateral resolution** depends on beam width and focus.
- [ ] **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$, unit = rayl.
- [ ] **Reflection coefficient** uses $(Z_2 - Z_1)^2 / (Z_2 + Z_1)^2$ — air–tissue gives ~99.9 % reflection (hence the gel!).
- [ ] **Doppler shift** $f_d = 2f_0 v \cos\theta / c$ — used in color flow and spectral Doppler.
- [ ] **PRF** limited by maximum imaging depth to avoid range ambiguity.
- [ ] **Subsystems to know cold:** Pulser, TGC, Beamformer, Scan Converter, Doppler Processor, Monitor.
- [ ] **Probe layers** (front to back): Acoustic lens → Matching layer → PZT → Backing → Housing.
- [ ] **Modes of imaging:** A-mode, B-mode, M-mode, Doppler (Color, Power, Spectral), 3-D/4-D.
- [ ] **Safety:** No ionizing radiation, ALARA principle for acoustic output, FDA limit $I_{\text{SPTA}} \leq 720 \, \text{mW/cm}^2$.
- [ ] **Use of coupling gel:** Eliminates air gap so ultrasound enters the body (otherwise it reflects back at the skin).
